673	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardId}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) do already exist at the
674	  presheaf level. This residual is being constructed \emph{directly}, mirroring the
675	  existing sheaf-level template app-for-app at the presheaf level (a Mathlib-gradient
676	  build, not a deferral to an upstream PR).

679	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(M, N\): the associator
680	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} consumes the comparison
681	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes_U N|_f\) on its sole consumer
682	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}, where the restriction is commuted
683	  past the \emph{sheafified} tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) \emph{before} any local
684	  trivialisation of \(M, N\) is introduced --- so the comparison is required for the

687	  \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) is genuinely on the critical path, and closing it is

690	  (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}) remains named only as a last-resort fallback should the
691	  presheaf-level adjunction itself bottom out; it is not the plan.

695	[Presheaf-level pushforward adjunction and strong-monoidal restriction (H1/H2 substrate)]
696	  \label{lem:presheaf_pushforward_adj_substrate}
697	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans,
698	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr,
699	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj,
700	        PresheafOfModules.isIso_of_isIso_app,
701	        PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective}
702	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
703	  These five presheaf-level declarations are the two ingredients on which the
704	  linchpin \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} rests; both are absent from Mathlib at
705	  the pinned commit and are supplied project-side.
706	
707	  \emph{H1 (the pushforward adjunction).} For a morphism \(\beta\) of presheaves of
708	  rings, \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\) are the
709	  presheaf-level de-sheafifications of Mathlib's sheaf-level
710	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
711	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr}\)
712	  (\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}),
713	  reproduced app-for-app one level down at the presheaf of modules. From them
714	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\) assembles the presheaf-level adjunction
715	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), the
716	  presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
717	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\). Pairing this against the
718	  existing \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\) through
719	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) yields the canonical isomorphism
720	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) of two left
721	  adjoints of the same pushforward --- exactly the residual identified in Step~3 of
722	  the proof of \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
723	
724	  \emph{H2 (strong-monoidal restriction).} \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_isIso\_app}\) is the
725	  sectionwise isomorphism criterion: a morphism of presheaves of modules is an
726	  isomorphism once each of its section maps is. Built on it,
727	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective}\) equips the presheaf-level
728	  restriction-of-scalars functor
729	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\alpha\) with a \emph{strong}-monoidal
730	  structure whenever the base-change comparison \(\alpha\) is \emph{sectionwise
731	  bijective} --- the presheaf-level lift of the ModuleCat-level H2 core
732	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}, assembled section-by-section via
733	  \(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\). It is to be distinguished from the
734	  module-level, ring-equivalence form
735	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv}\) already pinned in
736	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}: the present declaration is the
737	  \emph{presheaf}-level, \emph{bijective}-comparison form, the one the substrate lift
738	  actually consumes (where the comparison map
739	  \((\alpha.\mathtt{app}\,X).\mathtt{hom}\) is a bijective \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)
740	  morphism but is not syntactically a \((\,\cdot\,)\!.\mathtt{toRingHom}\)).
741	
742	  Together H1 and H2 are the two Mathlib-absent ingredients the linchpin needed; they
743	  are self-contained presheaf-level results and are natural upstream-PR candidates.
744	\end{lemma}

747	
748	\leanok
749	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with $\otimes$]
750	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
751	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}
752	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M, N\) be
753	  \(S\)-modules. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the tensor
754	  product: the canonical map
755	  \[
756	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N)
757	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
758	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \otimes_S N\bigr),
759	    \qquad a \otimes_R b \;\longmapsto\; a \otimes_S b,
760	  \]
761	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence. Equivalently, \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\) is
762	  \emph{strong} monoidal, not merely lax, when \(e\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}:
763	  base change along an isomorphism \(R \simeq S\) identifies the two ground rings, so
764	  the lax structure map of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) is invertible. The
765	  forward map is \(R\)-linear, but its inverse \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\)
766	  is only \emph{additive} (it is not \(S\)-linear), so the inverse is constructed as
767	  a homomorphism of additive groups and then verified to be the two-sided inverse of
768	  the forward \(R\)-linear map. This closes the strong-monoidal ingredient of
769	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, leaving the presheaf-level pushforward
770	  adjunction as the sole open residual there.
771	\end{lemma}

773	\begin{lemma}
774	
775	[Strong-monoidal upgrade of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along a ring isomorphism (ModuleCat-level H2 core)]
776	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}
777	  \lean{restrictScalars_isIso_μ, restrictScalars_isIso_ε,
778	        restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv,
779	        restrictScalars_isIso_μ_of_bijective,
780	        restrictScalars_isIso_ε_of_bijective}
781	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
782	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings and let
783	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom} :
784	  \mathtt{ModuleCat}\,S \to \mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R\) be the associated
785	  restriction-of-scalars functor, equipped with its canonical \emph{lax}-monoidal
786	  structure (the lax tensorator \(\mu\) and lax unit \(\varepsilon\) supplied by
787	  Mathlib). Then:
788	  \begin{enumerate}
789	    \item the lax tensorator
790	      \(\mu_{M, N} : \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e M \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e N
791	      \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M \otimes_S N)\) is an isomorphism
792	      (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\mu}\));
793	    \item the lax unit
794	      \(\varepsilon : \mathbf{1}_R \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(\mathbf{1}_S)\)
795	      is an isomorphism (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\varepsilon}\));
796	    \item consequently \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}\) is
797	      \emph{strong} monoidal: it carries the packaged structure
798	      \((\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
799	      (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv}\)).
800	  \end{enumerate}
801	  The same two invertibility statements hold, more flexibly, under the hypothesis that
802	  the underlying ring homomorphism is merely \emph{bijective} rather than literally of
803	  the form \(e.\mathtt{toRingHom}\) for a packaged ring equivalence \(e\)
804	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\mu\_of\_bijective}\),
805	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\varepsilon\_of\_bijective}\)); these bijective
806	  forms are the ones consumed by the presheaf-level lift, where the relevant
807	  comparison map \((\alpha.\mathtt{app}\,X).\mathtt{hom}\) is a bijective
808	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) morphism but is not syntactically a
809	  \((\,\cdot\,)\!.\mathtt{toRingHom}\).
810	\end{lemma}
811	
812	\begin{proof}
813	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
814	  Statements (1) and (2) are the categorical repackaging of
815	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}: when the ground-ring map is a ring
816	  \emph{isomorphism} the two ground rings are identified, so the lax structure maps
817	  \(\mu\) and \(\varepsilon\) of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) --- which are
818	  in general only one-directional --- become invertible. For (1) the inverse of
819	  \(\mu_{M, N}\) is the additive map \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\) of
820	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}, verified to be two-sided inverse to
821	  the canonical \(R\)-linear \(\mu_{M, N}\); for (2) the unit comparison
822	  \(\mathbf{1}_R = R \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e S\) is the additive identification
823	  induced by \(e\). Strong monoidality (3) is then the Mathlib upgrade of a
824	  lax-monoidal functor all of whose structure maps are isomorphisms
825	  (\(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\)), packaging \(\mu^{-1}, \varepsilon^{-1}\)
826	  into \((\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}).\mathtt{Monoidal}\).
827	  The bijective-hypothesis forms are proved identically, replacing ``\(e\) is a ring
828	  equivalence'' by ``the underlying ring homomorphism is bijective'' and reconstructing
829	  the inverse ground-ring map from bijectivity; this is what lets the presheaf-level
830	  lift feed in a section-wise-bijective comparison map directly.
831	
832	  These declarations constitute the \emph{ModuleCat-level H2}: strong
833	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along a ring isomorphism commutes with \(\otimes\). The
834	  one \emph{remaining} H2 step is the bounded, no-Mathlib-gap \emph{presheaf} lift:
835	  assembling \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\alpha).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
836	  section-by-section via \(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\), using a
837	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-iso\,\(\Rightarrow\)\,bijective bridge to invoke the
838	  bijective forms above at each section, together with the
839	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) iso-iff-app-iso reflection to lift section-wise
840	  invertibility to the presheaf level; once that lift is in place,
841	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) is monoidal. This presheaf H2 lift is bounded and
842	  has no Mathlib-absent ingredient; the sole genuinely Mathlib-absent piece of
843	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is the presheaf-level pushforward adjunction
844	  (``H1'').
845	\end{proof}

847	\begin{lemma}
848	
849	\leanok
850	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
851	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
852	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
853	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
854	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
855	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
856	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
857	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
858	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
859	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
860	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
861	\end{lemma}
862	
863	\begin{proof}
864	
865	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
866	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
867	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
868	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
869	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
870	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
871	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
872	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
873	\end{proof}

885	\begin{lemma}
886	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
887	
888	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
889	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
890	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
891	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
892	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
893	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
894	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
895	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
896	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
897	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
898	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
899	  left-whiskered morphism
900	  \[
901	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
902	  \]
903	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
904	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
905	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
906	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
907	
908	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
909	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
910	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
911	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability data for
912	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (route~(e)) are the flatness-free variants
913	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_W}\)
914	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}), whose stalkwise proof holds for an \emph{arbitrary}
915	  whiskering object \(F\) and never invokes the sectionwise-flatness hypothesis
916	  of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over
917	  \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the site, including non-affine opens
918	  --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility or local triviality, which are
919	  affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma is therefore retained as an
920	  independent, closed result, superseded on the critical path by the
921	  \(\mathtt{\_of\_W}\) variants of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
922	\end{lemma}
923	
924	\begin{proof}
925	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
926	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
927	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
928	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
929	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
930	
931	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
932	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
933	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
934	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
935	  \[
936	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
937	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
938	  \]
939	
940	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
941	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
942	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
943	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
944	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
945	
946	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
947	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
948	  linear maps (Mathlib
949	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
950	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
951	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
952	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
953	
954	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
955	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
956	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
957	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
958	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
959	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
960	\end{proof}

964	approach to the associator. The group law on iso-classes consumes only the

966	and the associator is now built directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms

968	whiskering / \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk apparatus is never required. This block is retained for the historical

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
56	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
57	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.
58	
59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on \(C \times_k T\) to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor \(D \subset C \times_k T\) over \(T\)
64	(an \(S\)-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), hence a \(T\)-point of the Hilbert
66	functor \(\Hilb_{C/k}(T)\), which is the special case \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
70	
71	\begin{lemma}\leanok
72	[Line bundle / Quot correspondence via the Abel map]
73	  \label{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}
74	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
75	  
76	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3, Def.~dfn:Abel (the Abel
77	  % map), and Thm.~th:repDiv (representability of relative effective
78	  % divisors by an open subscheme of \(\IHilb_{X/S}\)); arXiv:math/0504020,
79	  % pp.~25, 23 (read from
80	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1837-L1841 + L2135-L2145).
81	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
82	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:repDiv}
83	  %   Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat.  Then \(\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}\) is
84	  %  representable by an open subscheme \(\IDiv_{X/S}\) of the Hilbert scheme
85	  %  \(\IHilb_{X/S}\).
86	  %   \end{thm}"
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
88	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{dfn:Abel}
89	  %   The {\it Abel map\/} is the natural map of functors
90	  %      \(\)A_{X/S}(T)\:\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}(T)\to \Pic_{X/S}(T)\(\)
91	  %   defined by sending a relative effective divisor \(D\) on \(X_T/T\) to the
92	  %  sheaf \(\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)\).  The target \(\Pic_{X/S}\) may be replaced by any
93	  %  of its associated sheaves."
94	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3,
95	  Thm.~th:repDiv + Def.~dfn:Abel (cf.\ [Nitsure], ``Construction of Hilbert
96	  and Quot Schemes'', \S 1, where \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve, and let
98	  \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme. There is a natural map of presheaves on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\)
99	  \[
100	    A_{C/k} \;\colon\; \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et},
101	    \qquad D \;\longmapsto\; [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)],
102	  \]
103	  where \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of relative effective Cartier divisors
104	  on \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), and the target is the
105	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of
106	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}. The functor \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) is representable
107	  by an open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \subseteq \Hilb_{C/k}\) of the Hilbert scheme
108	  of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (with \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) the special case
109	  \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\) of the Quot functor). The Abel map is therefore
110	  representable by a morphism of \(k\)-schemes $A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to
111	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$.
112	\end{lemma}
113	
114	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
115	% L1843-L1866, proof of th:repDiv)
116	% "Set \(H:=\IHilb_{X/S}\), and let \(W\subset X\x H\) be the universal
117	% (closed) subscheme, and \(q\:W\to H\) the projection.  Let \(V\) denote the
118	% set of points \(w\in W\) at which \(W\) is an effective divisor. Plainly \(V\)
119	% is open in \(W\).  Set \(Z:=q(W-V)\).  Then \(Z\) is closed because \(q\) is
120	% proper.  Set \(U:=H-Z\).  Then \(U\) is open, and \(q^{-1}U\) is an effective
121	% divisor in \(X\x U\).  In fact, since \(q\) is flat, \(q^{-1}U\) is a relative
122	% effective divisor in \(X\x U/U\).
123	% [\dots]"
124	\begin{proof}
125	  
126	  By \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, the Hilbert functor $\Hilb_{C/k} =
127	  \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\( is representable by a \)k$-scheme; this uses
128	  \(C/k\) projective and flat (the smooth proper curve hypothesis is
129	  stronger). Let \(W \subseteq C \times_k \Hilb_{C/k}\) be the universal
130	  closed subscheme and \(q : W \to \Hilb_{C/k}\) the projection. The locus
131	  \(V \subseteq W\) where \(W\) is locally an effective Cartier divisor (i.e.\
132	  cut out by one regular element) is open. The complement \(W \setminus V\)
133	  is closed in \(W\), and the image $Z := q(W \setminus V) \subseteq
134	  \Hilb_{C/k}\( is closed because \)q\( is proper. Hence \)\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} :=
135	  \Hilb_{C/k} \setminus Z\( is open in \)\Hilb_{C/k}$ and represents the
136	  subfunctor of relative effective divisors: on \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) the
137	  restriction of \(W\) is flat and cut out by one regular element fiberwise,
138	  so by the standard relative-divisor criterion (Kleiman~\S 3
139	  Lem.~lm:ctn) it is a relative effective divisor on $C \times_k
140	  \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} / \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}$.
141	
142	  The Abel map sends a relative effective divisor \(D \subseteq C \times_k T\)
143	  to the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) := \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}\)
144	  (the dual of the ideal sheaf, which is invertible by the divisor
145	  hypothesis). The assignment is functorial in \(T\): pullback of an ideal
146	  sheaf along \(\mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\)
147	  takes the ideal of \(D\) to the ideal of \(D_{T'}\) (since \(D\) is flat over
148	  \(T\), the schematic inverse image \(D_{T'}\) is a relative effective
149	  divisor on \(C \times_k T'/T'\), again by Kleiman~\S 3 Lem.~lm:ctn), hence
150	  takes \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T'}(D_{T'})\).
151	  Composing with the canonical projection
152	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) of
153	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} and then with the
154	  \'etale sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} yields the
155	  natural map \(A_{C/k}(T) : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
156	\end{proof}

158	\section{The FGA representability theorem}
159	\label{sec:fga_pic_representability}
160	
161	We now state and outline the proof of Grothendieck's existence theorem
162	for the Picard scheme, in the specialisation needed for the Jacobian arm
163	of Route~A: \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral
164	curve. The general theorem (Kleiman \S 4 Thm.~th:main) covers any
165	\(X/S\) projective Zariski-locally over \(S\), flat, with integral geometric
166	fibers; we record the general statement and then specialise.
167	
168	\begin{theorem}\leanok
169	[FGA representability of the Picard scheme]
170	  \label{thm:fga_pic_representability}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
172	  
173	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main,
174	  % and Cor.~cor:algsch (specialisation to \(S = \Spec k\), \(X\) complete);
175	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~26, 35 (read from
176	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2155-L2166 + L2686-L2690).
177	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
178	  % "\begin{thm}[Main]\label{th:main}
179	  %   Assume \(f\:X\to S\) is projective Zariski locally over \(S\), and is flat
180	  %  with integral geometric fibers.
181	  %
182	  %  \tu{(1)} Then \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists, is separated and locally of finite
183	  %  type over \(S\), and represents \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\).
184	  %
185	  %  \tu{(2)} If also \(S\) is Noetherian and \(X/S\) is projective, then
186	  %  \(\IPic_{X/S}\) is a disjoint union of open
187	  %   subschemes, each an increasing union of open quasi-projective
188	  %  \(S\)-schemes.
189	  %   \end{thm}"
190	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
191	  % "\begin{sbscor}\label{cor:algsch}
192	  %    Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\), and \(X\) is complete.  Then
193	  %  \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists, represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\), and is a disjoint
194	  %  union of open quasi-projective subschemes.
195	  %   \end{sbscor}"
196	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main
197	  + Cor.~cor:algsch.}
198	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral curve
199	  over \(k\). Then the \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor
200	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} is
201	  representable by a \(k\)-scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), separated and locally of
202	  finite type over \(k\), which is a disjoint union of open
203	  quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes, each an increasing union of open
204	  quasi-projective subschemes. The scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is called the
205	  \emph{Picard scheme} of \(C/k\).
206	\end{theorem}
207	
208	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
209	% L2168-L2366, proof of th:main; long and is broken into the four-step
210	% sketch below per the project's structural restatement convention. The
211	% verbatim quote of the opening reduction step is:)
212	% "By~\cite[(0, 4.5.5), p.~106]{EGAG}, it is a local matter on \(S\) to
213	% represent a Zariski sheaf on the category of \(S\)-schemes.  Moreover, it
214	% is also a local matter on \(S\) to prove that an \(S\)-scheme is separated
215	% and locally of finite type.  Hence, in order to prove (1), we may assume
216	% \(S\) is Noetherian and \(X/S\) is projective.
217	% [\dots]
218	% Plainly an \(S\)-scheme is separated if it is a disjoint union of
219	% separated open subschemes, or if it is an increasing union of
220	% separated open subschemes.  Hence (1) follows from (2)."
221	% (subsequent steps quoted inline in the project-notation proof below).
222	\begin{proof}
223	  
224	  We follow Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:main, specialised to \(S = \Spec k\) and
225	  \(X = C\). The proof proceeds in four conceptual steps; the underlying
226	  algebraic engine is the Quot scheme of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
227	  and the \'etale-sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
228	  together with the line-bundle / Quot correspondence of
229	  \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}.
230	
231	  \emph{Step 1: stratify by Hilbert polynomial.}
232	  For each polynomial \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\), let $P^\phi \subseteq
233	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\( be the \'etale subsheaf whose \)T$-points are
234	  represented by invertible sheaves \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) with
235	  \(\chi(C_t, \mathcal{L}_t^{-1}(n)) = \phi(n)\) for all \(t \in T\) (the
236	  Hilbert polynomial relative to the chosen polarisation
237	  \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on the projective curve \(C\)). The \(P^\phi\) form a
238	  disjoint open cover of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (openness via Kleiman's
239	  flat-base-change argument; cf.\ EGA III 7.9.11). It suffices to
240	  represent each \(P^\phi\) by an increasing union of open quasi-projective
241	  \(k\)-schemes.
242	
243	  \emph{Step 2: bound twists by \(m\)-regularity.}
244	  Given \(\phi\), fix \(m\) large enough that the \(T\)-points of \(P^\phi\) are
245	  representable by \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) satisfying
246	  \(R^i \pi_{T*} \mathcal{L}(n) = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\), \(n \ge m\). Twisting
247	  by \(\mathcal{O}_C(m)\) defines an automorphism of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
248	  that carries \(P^\phi\) to \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) for \(\phi_0(n) := \phi(m + n)\).
249	  Hence it suffices to represent each \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) by a quasi-projective
250	  \(k\)-scheme.
251	
252	  \emph{Step 3: factor through the Abel map.}
253	  Consider the Abel map \(A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
254	  of \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}. The source \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
255	  is representable by an open subscheme of \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) (and hence by an
256	  open subscheme of the Quot scheme of \cref{thm:quot_representable}), and
257	  is quasi-projective over \(k\). Pulling back along
258	  \(P^{\phi_0}_0 \hookrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) yields an open
259	  subscheme \(Z := P^{\phi_0}_0 \times_{\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}} \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
260	  of \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\), which is again quasi-projective. The projection
261	  \(\alpha : Z \to P^{\phi_0}_0\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves:
262	  given a \(T\)-point \(\lambda\) of \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) represented by an \'etale
263	  cover \(T' \to T\) and an invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}'\) on $C \times_k
264	  T'\( with \)H^1(C_{t'}, \mathcal{L}'_{t'}) = 0$, the projective bundle
265	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to T'\) associated to
266	  \(\mathcal{Q} := \pi_{T'*}\mathcal{L}'\) is smooth over \(T'\); an \'etale
267	  cover \(T_1 \to T'\) admits a \(T'\)-map \(T_1 \to \mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q})\)
268	  (EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii)) whose composition with the universal divisor map
269	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to Z\) produces the required \(T_1\)-point of \(Z\).
270	  Moreover, the first projection \(Z \times_{P^{\phi_0}_0} Z \to Z\) is
271	  smooth and proper.
272	
273	  \emph{Step 4: descend by the smooth-proper quotient lemma.}
274	  Apply Kleiman~\S 4 Lem.~lm:qt: given a surjection \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
275	  of \'etale sheaves with \(Z\) quasi-projective, \(R := Z \times_P Z\)
276	  representable, and the first projection \(R \to Z\) smooth and proper,
277	  \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective scheme. In our case
278	  \(P = P^{\phi_0}_0\), \(Z\) and \(R\) as above, so \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) is
279	  quasi-projective. Reassembling over \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\) and
280	  over \(m\), the disjoint union of representable \(P^\phi\) pieces represents
281	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\). The total scheme is separated (as a disjoint
282	  union of separated quasi-projectives) and locally of finite type. This
283	  is Kleiman~\S 4 Cor.~cor:algsch in the \(S = \Spec k\),
284	  \(X = C\) complete case.
285	\end{proof}
286	
287	\subsection{The smooth-proper quotient lemma}
288	\label{subsec:fga_pic_qt}
289	
290	The structural lemma underlying step 4 above is recorded separately
291	because it is consumed verbatim in the assembly proof: it is the
292	abstract content allowing the descent from \(Z = \mathrm{Div}^{\phi_0}_0\) to the
293	representing Picard piece.
294	
295	\begin{lemma}\leanok
296	[Smooth-proper quotient of \'etale sheaves]
297	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}
298	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
299	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt;
300	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~31 (read from
301	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2368-L2375).
302	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
303	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lm:qt}
304	  %   Let \(\alpha\:Z\to P\) be a map of \'etale sheaves, and set \(R:=Z\x_PZ\).
305	  %  Assume \(\alpha\) is a surjection, \(Z\) is representable by a
306	  %  quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, \(R\) is representable by an \(S\)-scheme, and
307	  %  the first projection \(R\to Z\) is representable by a smooth and proper
308	  %  map.  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, and
309	  %  \(\alpha\) is representable by a smooth map."
310	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt.}
311	  Let \(\alpha : Z \to P\) be a morphism of \'etale sheaves on \((\Sch/k)\).
312	  Set \(R := Z \times_P Z\). Suppose:
313	  \begin{enumerate}
314	    \item \(\alpha\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves;
315	    \item \(Z\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme;
316	    \item \(R\) is representable by a \(k\)-scheme;
317	    \item the first projection \(R \to Z\) is representable by a smooth
318	      and proper map.
319	  \end{enumerate}
320	  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme, and \(\alpha\)
321	  is representable by a smooth map.
322	\end{lemma}
323	
324	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
325	% L2377-L2417, proof of lm:qt; reproduced verbatim because it is the
326	% structural heart of step 4.)
327	% "To lighten the notation, let \(Z\) and \(R\) denote the
328	% corresponding schemes as well.  Since the structure map \(Z\to S\) is
329	% quasi-projective, it is separated; whence, the first projection
330	% \(Z\x_SZ\to Z\) is too.  But the first projection \(R\to Z\) is proper, and
331	% factors naturally through a map \(h\:R\to Z\x_SZ\).  Hence \(h\) is proper.
332	% But \(h\) is a monomorphism; that is, \(h\) is injective on \(T\)-points.
333	% Therefore, \(h\) is a closed embedding by \cite[8.11.5]{EGAIV3}.
334	%
335	% Plainly, for each \(S\)-scheme \(T\), the subset $R(T)\subset
336	% Z(T)\x_{S(T)}Z(T)$ is the graph of an equivalence relation.  Also, the
337	% map of schemes \(R\to Z\) is flat and proper, and \(Z\) is a
338	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme.  It follows that there exist a
339	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat and projective map
340	% \(Z\to Q\) such that \(R=Z\x_QZ\).  (In other words, a flat and proper
341	% equivalence relation on a quasi-projective scheme is effective.)
342	% [\dots]
343	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is flat, it is smooth if (and only if) its fibers are
344	% smooth.  But these fibers are, up to extension of the ground field, the
345	% same as those of \(R\to Z\).  And \(R\to Z\) is smooth by hypothesis.  Thus
346	% \(Z\to Q\) is smooth.
347	%
348	% It remains to see that \(Q\) represents \(P\).  First, observe that \(Z\to Q\)
349	% is (represents) a surjection of \'etale sheaves.
350	% [\dots]
351	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves, \(Q\) is, in the
352	% category of \'etale sheaves, the coequalizer of the pair of maps
353	% \(R\rightrightarrows Z\) by Exercise~REF below, which is an
354	% elementary exercise in general Grothendieck topology.  By the same
355	% exercise, \(P\) too is the coequalizer of this pair of maps.  But, in any
356	% category, the coequalizer is unique up to unique isomorphism.  Thus \(Q\)
357	% represents \(P\)."
358	\begin{proof}
359	
360	\leanok
361	  By (ii) the structure map \(Z \to \Spec k\) is quasi-projective, hence
362	  separated, so the first projection \(Z \times_k Z \to Z\) is separated.
363	  By (iv) the first projection \(R \to Z\) is proper; it factors through
364	  \(h : R \to Z \times_k Z\) over the diagonal embedding (the second
365	  component is the second projection of \(R \to Z \times_P Z\)). The map
366	  \(h\) is therefore proper, and a monomorphism (because \(R\) is the graph
367	  of an equivalence relation on \(Z\), hence injective on \(T\)-points).
368	  By EGA IV 8.11.5, a proper monomorphism is a closed immersion: \(h\) is a
369	  closed embedding.
370	
371	  Hence \(R \hookrightarrow Z \times_k Z\) is a flat (by (iv)) and proper
372	  equivalence relation on the quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Z\). By the
373	  theory of flat-and-proper effective equivalence relations on
374	  quasi-projective schemes (Altman--Kleiman; the construction places
375	  the graph \(R \subseteq Z \times_k Z\) inside the universal subscheme of
376	  the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{Z/k}\) and descends to a closed subscheme
377	  \(Q \subseteq \Hilb_{Z/k}\) representing the quotient), there exist a
378	  quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat, projective map
379	  \(Z \to Q\) with \(R = Z \times_Q Z\).
380	
381	  The map \(Z \to Q\) is flat and proper; its fibers, up to field
382	  extension, coincide with those of \(R \to Z\), which are smooth by
383	  hypothesis. Hence \(Z \to Q\) is smooth.
384	
385	  Finally, \(Z \to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves (an \'etale
386	  cover of any \(T\)-point of \(Q\) pulls back to a \(T'\)-point of \(Z\), by
387	  EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii) applied to \(Z \times_Q T \to T\)). In the category
388	  of \'etale sheaves, both \(Q\) and \(P\) are coequalisers of the pair
389	  \(R \rightrightarrows Z\); the coequaliser is unique up to unique
390	  isomorphism, so \(Q\) represents \(P\). Smoothness of \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
391	  is the smoothness of \(Z \to Q\) just shown.
392	\end{proof}
393	
394	\section{Group-scheme structure}
395	\label{sec:fga_pic_group_structure}
396	
397	The Picard scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) inherits an abelian group structure from
398	the tensor product of invertible sheaves on \(C \times_k T\). The relative
399	Picard presheaf \(T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T\) is already an abelian
400	group at every \(T\) (the quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup),
401	functorially in \(T\); this lifts to the \'etale sheafification, and then
402	to a group-scheme structure on the representing scheme by Yoneda.
403	
404	\begin{theorem}\leanok
405	[\(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group scheme]
406	  \label{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}
407	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
408	  
409	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs (the relative
410	  % Picard functor takes values in abelian groups) and \S 4 surrounding
411	  % text (group-scheme structure on \(\IPic_{X/S}\) via tensor product);
412	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~17, 26 (read from
413	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318 + L2057-L2069).
414	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
415	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
416	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
417	  %      \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
418	  %   Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
419	  %  topologies  by
420	  %      \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
421	  %      \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
422	  %   \end{dfn}"
423	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
424	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
425	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~REF
426	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
427	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by \(\IPic_{X/S}\).  Moreover, we say simply that the
428	  %  Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists."
429	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs +
430	  \S 4, Def.~df:Psch (the Picard scheme).}
431	  Let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
432	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. Then \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is canonically an
433	  abelian group scheme over \(k\): the tensor product
434	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}]\)
435	  and inverse \(-[\mathcal{L}] := [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]\) on
436	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) (well defined because both operations
437	  preserve isomorphism classes and descend through the subgroup
438	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined})
439	  are natural in \(T\), hence by Yoneda are represented by morphisms
440	  \(m : \Pic_{C/k} \times_k \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\),
441	  \(i : \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\), and
442	  \(e : \Spec k \to \Pic_{C/k}\) (the trivial line bundle class) satisfying
443	  the abelian-group axioms. In particular, \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group
444	  scheme locally of finite type.
445	\end{theorem}
446	
447	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- Kleiman \S 2--4 do not isolate a
448	% separate proof block for the group-scheme structure: it is implicit in
449	% the fact that \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) takes values in abelian groups (df:Pfs)
450	% and that representability transfers algebraic operations from set-valued
451	% functors to scheme morphisms by Yoneda. The structural restatement below
452	% records the standard transfer.
453	\begin{proof}
454	  
455	  The relative Picard functor \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) of
456	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} is already abelian-group
457	  valued, with sum $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] = [\mathcal{L} \otimes
458	  \mathcal{M}]\(, inverse \)-[\mathcal{L}] = [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]$, and unit
459	  \(0 = [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]\); these operations descend through
460	  the quotient by \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) because \(\pi_T^*\) is itself a
461	  group homomorphism. The \'etale sheafification step of
462	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} preserves the abelian-group structure
463	  on values, because sheafification of an abelian-presheaf is again an
464	  abelian-sheaf (the sheafification functor commutes with finite products
465	  in the value category).
466	
467	  By \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is
468	  represented by the scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The Yoneda embedding is fully
469	  faithful and preserves limits, so the natural transformations
470	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \times \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to
471	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (multiplication),
472	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (inverse), and the
473	  unit map \(h_{\Spec k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) pick out unique
474	  morphisms of representing schemes \(m\), \(i\), \(e\) as displayed. The
475	  abelian-group axioms (associativity, commutativity, identity,
476	  inverse) hold at the level of \(T\)-points functorially in \(T\), hence
477	  hold on the representing scheme by Yoneda. The local-finite-type
478	  property is part of \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}.
479	\end{proof}
480	
481	\section{The Picard scheme: the named declaration}
482	\label{sec:fga_pic_named_definition}
483	
484	The two preceding theorems are existence statements. We record the
485	named declaration corresponding to the Lean target.
486	
487	\begin{definition}\leanok
488	[The Picard scheme]
489	  \label{def:pic_scheme}
490	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
491	  
492	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch;
493	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26 (read from
494	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2057-L2062).
495	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
496	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
497	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~REF
498	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
499	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by \(\IPic_{X/S}\).  Moreover, we say simply that the
500	  %  Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists."
501	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch.}
502	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
503	  \(k\). The \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is the \(k\)-scheme representing
504	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\), supplied by
505	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, equipped with the abelian-group-scheme
506	  structure of \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}. The scheme is separated,
507	  locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of open
508	  quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes.
509	\end{definition}
510	
511	\section{Lean encoding}
512	\label{sec:fga_pic_lean_encoding}
513	
514	The Lean target file is the new
515	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean}, sibling to
516	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
517	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) and
518	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}
519	(\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}). The blueprint declarations of
520	this chapter correspond to Lean targets as follows.
521	
522	\begin{itemize}
523	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
524	    (\cref{def:pic_scheme}) is a structural constant naming the scheme
525	    representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\); it is the witness extracted
526	    from the \texttt{Functor.RepresentableBy} produced by
527	    \texttt{representable} below.
528	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
529	    (\cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}) is the morphism of
530	    \(k\)-schemes \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) sending a
531	    relative effective divisor to its associated invertible sheaf.
532	    The construction uses the Hilbert / Quot scheme produced by
533	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (named
534	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotScheme} per the directive's
535	    sibling-chapter naming), restricts to the open subscheme of relative
536	    effective divisors via the Kleiman~\S 3 effective-divisor open
537	    criterion, and composes with the quotient map to
538	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\).
539	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
540	    (\cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}) is the Altman--Kleiman
541	    descent of a flat-and-proper equivalence relation $R \subseteq Z
542	    \times_k Z\( on a quasi-projective \)k\(-scheme \)Z$ to a
543	    quasi-projective quotient \(Q\), packaged in the form that consumes
544	    a \(\Functor.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\)-witness for \(Z\) and produces
545	    one for the coequalising sheaf.
546	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
547	    (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}) is the assembly: from the
548	    \texttt{abelMap}, the \texttt{QuotScheme} representability witness,
549	    and the \texttt{rel\_pic\_etale\_sheafification} of
550	    \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}, build the
551	    \(\Functor.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\)-witness for
552	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) via the four steps of the proof above
553	    (Hilbert-polynomial stratification, \(m\)-regularity bound, Abel-map
554	    factorisation, smooth-proper quotient).
555	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
556	    (\cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}) is the
557	    \texttt{CommGroupObject} (or
558	    \texttt{AddCommGrp\_Object}) instance on \texttt{PicScheme} obtained
559	    by Yoneda transport of the abelian-group structure on
560	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\).
561	\end{itemize}

564	top of the QuotScheme engine, the relative-Picard \'etale sheafification,
565	and the line-bundle pullback machinery already in place. The single

742	invertible sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) = \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}$.
743	This is the substantive content of
744	\cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}.
745	
746	\paragraph{Mathlib prerequisites.}
747	\begin{itemize}
748	  \item Natural transformation \texttt{${\rightsquigarrow}$} of
749	    functors, \texttt{Nonempty}: \emph{exists}.
750	  \item Dual of an ideal sheaf $\mathcal{I}_D^{-1}$ as an invertible
751	    sheaf for a relative effective Cartier divisor: \emph{partial}.
752	    Mathlib has dual modules and dual sheaves but the specific link
753	    "ideal of a relative effective Cartier divisor is invertible and
754	    its dual is the associated line bundle" is a project-internal
755	    bridge.
756	  \item Functoriality of the assignment under pullback (Kleiman \S3
757	    Lem.~\texttt{lm:ctn}): \emph{needs-bridge}. Pullback of relative
758	    effective divisors along base-change is standard for flat-over-
759	    base divisors; Mathlib's coverage of this for the scheme-theoretic
760	    relative setting is incomplete.
761	\end{itemize}
762	
763	\paragraph{Route~C substrate dependency.} None: the Abel map is a
764	construction at the level of line bundles and divisors, not a
765	consequence of Riemann--Roch. (Riemann--Roch tells us that the Abel
766	map for a fixed degree-$g$ pole-set is surjective onto $\Pic^g_{C/k}$;
767	this is a downstream consequence, not a prerequisite for defining the
768	map.)
769	
770	\paragraph{Closure recipe (iter-199+).} The component-wise definition
771	of $A_{C/k}(T)$ is: given $D \in \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T)$, set
772	$A_{C/k}(T)(D) := [\mathcal{I}_D^{-1}]$ in
773	$\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$. Naturality follows from the pullback
774	formula for the dual ideal sheaf. The Lean encoding is a sequence of
775	three constructions: (a) extract the ideal sheaf of a relative
776	effective Cartier divisor, (b) dualise to get the line bundle,
777	(c) pass to the \'etale sheafified class. Each step is independent of
778	Riemann--Roch and depends only on Sorries~1 and~2 having committed to
779	a semantic witness for \texttt{picSharp} and \texttt{divFunctor}.
780	
781	\paragraph{Closure-order rank.} \textbf{Rank 2} (depends on Sorries~1
782	and~2; no Riemann--Roch substrate).

797	\paragraph{Mathematical content.} The smooth-proper quotient lemma
798	of \cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}: given a surjection
799	$\alpha : Z \to P$ of \'etale sheaves on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$ with $Z$
800	quasi-projective, $R := Z \times_P Z$ representable, and the first
801	projection $R \to Z$ smooth and proper, the quotient $P$ is
802	representable by a quasi-projective $k$-scheme. The proof packages
803	the Altman--Kleiman effective-equivalence-relation theorem for
804	flat-and-proper relations on quasi-projective schemes.
805	
806	\paragraph{Mathlib prerequisites.}
807	\begin{itemize}
808	  \item \texttt{Functor.IsRepresentable},
809	    \texttt{Functor.RepresentableBy}, \texttt{Limits.pullback}:
810	    \emph{exists}.
811	  \item \texttt{Smooth}, \texttt{IsProper} typeclasses on scheme
812	    morphisms: \emph{exists}.
813	  \item EGA IV~8.11.5 "proper monomorphism is a closed immersion":
814	    \emph{missing}. Mathlib has closed immersions, monomorphisms of
815	    schemes, and properness, but the EGA combination lemma is not yet
816	    isolated as a named result.
817	  \item Altman--Kleiman effective-equivalence-relation theorem
818	    (flat-and-proper equivalence relation on a quasi-projective
819	    scheme is effective, with quasi-projective quotient):
820	    \emph{missing}. This is the structural heart of the lemma and is
821	    not in Mathlib in any form.
822	  \item Coequaliser uniqueness in the category of \'etale sheaves:
823	    \emph{exists} (general categorical fact, uniqueness up to unique
824	    isomorphism of coequalisers in any category).
825	\end{itemize}

828	structural fact about quasi-projective schemes and \'etale sheaves,

832	Altman--Kleiman, "Compactifying the Picard scheme", and the

1023	formalized.} The construction below realizes a \emph{full} symmetric monoidal
1024	category on all of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) by instantiating Mathlib's
1025	localization-of-monoidal-categories API. It was the original plan for supplying the
1026	associator. The relative Picard group law, however, is a structure on
1027	\emph{isomorphism classes} of invertible sheaves and consumes only the
1028	\emph{existence} of the associator, unitor and braiding isomorphisms
1029	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, built by hand as the scheme-theoretic
1030	analogue of Mathlib's \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), not off a coherent monoidal
1031	category's \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)). The associator
1032	is now obtained directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
1033	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
1034	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), which uses \emph{no} \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\),
1035	\emph{no} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\), and \emph{no} stalk machinery. Accordingly
1036	the whiskering-stability lemmas and the stalk apparatus of this section are
1037	\emph{vestigial} and must not be re-attempted. The material is kept for the historical record only.
1038	
1039	The monoidal structure on the substrate is realized by \emph{instantiating}
1040	Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
1041	(\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}): apply
1042	\(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) to the already-monoidal
1043	presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) and the
1044	sheafification localizer \(J.W\). The single new input the API requires is the
1045	instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), whose two fields are the
1046	whiskering-stability lemmas below.
1047	
1048	\paragraph{Two-tier strategy.} The group law is assembled by a \emph{two-tier}
1049	strategy, of which this route~(e) is the second tier. The \textbf{primary} tier
1050	builds the commutative group directly on isomorphism classes of \emph{locally
1051	trivial} (invertible) sheaves (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), mirroring
1052	Mathlib's ring-level Picard group \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) /
1053	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). On top of
1054	the locally-trivial-scoped associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors
1055	\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso} (all
1056	either already proved or locally-trivial-scoped and assembled), it needs only the
1057	restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and the
1058	dual/inverse \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}; it uses \emph{no}
1059	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \emph{no} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\), \emph{no}
1060	\(\mathtt{Skeleton}\), and in particular never invokes the stalk--tensor commutation
1061	(d.2). The \textbf{fallback} tier is the full \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to
1062	\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category on all of \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\)
1063	(this section); it is deferred because its load-bearing whiskering field
1064	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, for an arbitrary whisker object, needs
1065	(d.2) --- the stalk commuting with the relative tensor over a \emph{varying} ring, a
1066	Mathlib-absent ingredient --- and is retained only for the full
1067	monoidal generality on arbitrary modules, which the group law does not consume. The
1068	load-bearing local-injectivity half of \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) is thus the sole
1069	remaining open obligation of the \emph{fallback} tier only.

1078	\begin{lemma}
1079	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.

1081	[The $R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x$-linear stalk map of a morphism of presheaves of modules]
1082	  \label{lem:stalk_linear_map}
1083	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1084	  Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(R\) a presheaf of commutative rings on
1085	  \(X\), and \(x \in X\) a point. For a morphism \(g : M \to N\) of presheaves of
1086	  \(R\)-modules, the induced map on stalks
1087	  \[
1088	    g_x \;\colon\; M_x \;\longrightarrow\; N_x
1089	  \]
1090	  is \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\)), and is
1091	  characterised on germs by
1092	  \(g_x(\mathrm{germ}_x\,s) = \mathrm{germ}_x(g_U\,s)\)
1093	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_germ}\)). When the underlying map of
1094	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-valued stalks is an isomorphism, \(g_x\) is bijective
1095	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\)); in that case it bundles to
1096	  an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear equivalence
1097	  \(M_x \xrightarrow{\sim} N_x\) (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso}\)).
1098	
1099	  These four declarations are the \emph{(d.1)-partial implementation} --- the
1100	  linearity half of ingredient (d.1) of
1101	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} --- consumed by the
1102	  \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step in the proof of that lemma. They build on the
1103	  stalk module structure of \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk};
1104	  the linearity packaging itself is project-original.
1105	\end{lemma}
1106	
1107	\begin{proof}
1108	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1109	  The stalk \(M_x = \mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) carries its
1110	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure from
1111	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk}. The underlying additive map
1112	  \(g_x\) on stalks is the filtered colimit of the section maps \(g_U\); since
1113	  each section map is \(R(U)\)-linear and the scalar action on the stalk is the
1114	  colimit of the section actions, \(g_x\) respects the
1115	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-action, which gives the linear map together with its
1116	  germ characterisation. Bijectivity is read off the underlying
1117	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk isomorphism (a bijective linear map is a linear
1118	  equivalence), and bundling the inverse yields the linear equivalence.
1119	\end{proof}

1124	associator (route~(e)). The group law on iso-classes consumes only the

1129	including both its PRIMARY and FALLBACK proof routes and the stalk ingredients

1133	\begin{lemma}
1134	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.

1136	\leanok
1137	[Local injectivity of a whiskered localizer morphism (stalkwise, flatness-free)]
1138	  \label{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1139	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1140	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
1141	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the sheafification localizer (the locally
1142	  bijective morphisms). Concretely the site carries the
1143	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) property for \(\mathtt{Ab}\), so \(J.W\) is
1144	  exactly the class of \(J\)-locally bijective morphisms; this is recorded as a
1145	  typeclass hypothesis \([J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}]\) on
1146	  the statement. Let \(F\) be an \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of
1147	  \(R\)-modules, and let \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of
1148	  \(R\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
1149	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R).\mathrm{map}\,g\) lies in \(J.W\). Then the
1150	  left-whiskered morphism
1151	  \[
1152	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
1153	  \]
1154	  is \emph{\(J\)-locally injective}. No flatness and no local-triviality
1155	  hypothesis on \(F\) is required.
1156	
1157	  This is the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\)-field content (the injectivity half) of the
1158	  instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}), the load-bearing
1159	  field of the \emph{full-generality} route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path
1160	  (arbitrary \(F\)). For the relative Picard group law, however, the whisker object
1161	  \(F\) supplied by the sole consumer --- the locally-trivial-scoped associator
1162	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- is always \emph{locally trivial}
1163	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), and in that case local injectivity
1164	  of \(F \triangleleft g\) admits a markedly simpler, stalk-free proof, given as the
1165	  PRIMARY route below. The general stalkwise argument (ingredients (d.1)/(d.2)) is
1166	  retained only as the FALLBACK, required if the full route~(e) monoidal category on
1167	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) is later wanted; the locally-trivial
1168	  primary route already suffices for the group law.
1169	\end{lemma}
1170	
1171	\begin{proof}
1172	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map,
1173	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1174	
1175	  \emph{PRIMARY route (\(F\) locally trivial --- the consumer's case; sheaf-level,
1176	  no stalks, no (d.2)).} Suppose \(F\) is locally trivial
1177	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), which is exactly the hypothesis its
1178	  sole consumer --- the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- supplies.
1179	  \(J\)-local injectivity of a morphism is \emph{local on a covering family}: it
1180	  suffices to produce a cover of \(X\) on each member of which \(F \triangleleft g\)
1181	  restricts to a locally injective morphism. Take a trivialising open cover
1182	  \(\{V\}\) of \(X\) for \(F\), so that \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) on each \(V\).
1183	  On such a \(V\):
1184	  \begin{itemize}
1185	    \item the substrate--restriction compatibility \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1186	      gives a canonical isomorphism
1187	      \[
1188	        (F \triangleleft g)\big|_V \;\cong\; (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)
1189	      \]
1190	      --- restriction along the open immersion \(V \hookrightarrow X\) commutes with
1191	      \(\otimes\), and the whiskering \(F \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g\)
1192	      is \(\otimes\) with an identity, so it restricts to \(\mathrm{id}_{F|_V}
1193	      \otimes (g|_V) = (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)\);
1194	    \item the trivialisation \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) together with the left
1195	      unitor \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_V \otimes_V (-) \cong
1196	      (-)\)) identifies \((F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V) \cong g|_V\).
1197	  \end{itemize}
1198	  Composing, \((F \triangleleft g)|_V\) is isomorphic to \(g|_V\). Now \(g \in J.W\),
1199	  and membership in \(J.W\) (local bijectivity) is stable under restriction along an
1200	  open immersion, so \(g|_V \in J.W\); in particular \(g|_V\) is \(J\)-locally
1201	  injective. An isomorphism preserves local injectivity, hence \((F \triangleleft
1202	  g)|_V\) is locally injective on \(V\). As the \(\{V\}\) cover \(X\) and local
1203	  injectivity is local on the cover, \(F \triangleleft g\) is \(J\)-locally
1204	  injective. \emph{No stalks and no (d.2) stalk--tensor commutation enter}; the
1205	  single comparison lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (a presheaf-level
1206	  comparison, decomposed into its two open-immersion base-change halves) carries the
1207	  argument, and thereby moves \emph{onto} the critical path.
1208	
1209	  This is \emph{not} the section-level route that tensors a bare section map \(g(V)\)
1210	  --- only injective --- and stalls on a \(\mathrm{Tor}_1\)/flatness obligation. Here
1211	  the argument is sheaf-level and uses that \(g\) is locally \emph{bijective} (a
1212	  \(J.W\) morphism), reduced through the unitor to \(g|_V\) (again a \(J.W\)
1213	  morphism); a mere injection is never tensored, so no flatness is needed.
1214	
1215	  \emph{FALLBACK route (arbitrary \(F\); stalkwise, required only for the
1216	  full-generality route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path).} For an
1217	  \emph{arbitrary} (not necessarily locally trivial) \(F\), local injectivity is
1218	  proved stalkwise, by the argument Mathlib uses for fixed-base presheaves in
1219	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\)
1220	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}), ported to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). A
1221	  morphism in \(J.W\) is, on the topological site of \(X\), a \emph{stalkwise
1222	  isomorphism}: at each point \(x\) the induced map on stalks \(g_x\) is an
1223	  isomorphism. The presheaf-of-modules tensor is sectionwise, so it commutes with
1224	  the stalk (a filtered colimit), giving
1225	  \[
1226	    (F \triangleleft g)_x
1227	      \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes_{R_x} g_x
1228	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \;\longrightarrow\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
1229	  \]
1230	  Tensoring an isomorphism \(g_x\) by the identity \(\mathrm{id}_{F_x}\) yields an
1231	  isomorphism --- no flatness is needed, since \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely
1232	  injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, in particular
1233	  locally bijective, in particular locally injective, for \emph{any} \(F\).
1234	
1235	  The stalkwise computation rests on three ingredients with distinct status at the
1236	  pinned commit:
1237	  \begin{enumerate}
1238	    \item[(d.1-done)] the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear stalk-map packaging
1239	      (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) and its companions, \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}):
1240	      a morphism of presheaves of modules induces an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear
1241	      map on stalks, characterised on germs, that becomes a linear equivalence once
1242	      the underlying \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map is an isomorphism. This is built
1243	      project-side, axiom-clean, and furnishes the \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step
1244	      above.
1245	    \item[(d.1-bridge)] (remaining) the site-\(J.W\) \(\Longleftrightarrow\)
1246	      stalkwise-iso characterisation on \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\): that a morphism lies
1247	      in \(J.W\) iff its stalk maps are isomorphisms at every point. The concrete
1248	      Mathlib assembly route uses the \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) structure
1249	      together with the topological stalk criteria --- local injectivity
1250	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) injectivity on stalks and local surjectivity
1251	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) surjectivity on stalks --- bridging the site-level
1252	      \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsLocallyInjective}\) / \(\mathtt{IsLocallySurjective}\)
1253	      to the \(\mathtt{TopCat.Presheaf}\) stalk versions. This half is still
1254	      Mathlib-absent at the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
1255	    \item[(d.2)] (remaining) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
1256	      \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk (a filtered colimit) with the relative
1257	      module-presheaf tensor, under which \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) is identified
1258	      with \(\mathrm{lTensor}\,F_x\,(g_x) = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) (using
1259	      that \(\mathtt{tensorLeft}\) / \(\mathtt{tensorRight}\) preserve filtered
1260	      colimits over a module category). This is genuinely Mathlib-absent over a
1261	      \emph{varying} ring and is the largest remaining piece.
1262	  \end{enumerate}
1263	  Mathlib supplies the analogues for presheaves valued in a fixed monoidal concrete
1264	  category, but not for modules over a varying ring; porting (d.1-bridge) and (d.2)
1265	  to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) is the remaining work. Once (d.2) lands, the
1266	  conclusion is immediate and flatness-free: by
1267	  \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\) the stalk map \(g_x\) is a
1268	  linear equivalence, and the linear-equivalence \(\mathrm{lTensor}\) by \(F_x\)
1269	  carries it to an isomorphism, finishing the proof.
1270	
1271	  This stalkwise fallback is required \emph{only} if the full-generality
1272	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) route~(e) --- the
1273	  monoidal category on \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), for an
1274	  arbitrary whisker object \(F\) --- is later wanted. For the relative Picard group
1275	  law the whisker objects are locally trivial, and the PRIMARY route above suffices,
1276	  trading the Mathlib-absent (d.2) for the single comparison
1277	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1278	\end{proof}

1288	\begin{lemma}
1289	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
1290	[Flatness-free whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
1291	  \label{lem:whisker_of_W}
1292	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1293	  With notation as in \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: for an
1294	  \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of \(R\)-modules \(F\) and a morphism \(g\) whose
1295	  underlying additive-presheaf map lies in \(J.W\), both whiskerings again lie in
1296	  \(J.W\):
1297	  \[
1298	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; F \triangleleft g \in J.W,
1299	    \qquad
1300	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; g \triangleright F \in J.W .
1301	  \]
1302	  These two statements are precisely the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) and
1303	  \(\mathtt{whiskerRight}\) data required by the class
1304	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) for
1305	  the morphism property \(J.W\); together they furnish the instance
1306	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) of \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}. No flatness and no
1307	  local triviality is used --- they are the flatness-free replacements for the
1308	  flat variants of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}.
1309	\end{lemma}
1310	
1311	\begin{proof}
1312	  \uses{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1313	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of \(J.W\), it suffices to show
1314	  \(F \triangleleft g\) is both locally injective and locally surjective.
1315	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is free and needs no hypothesis on \(F\): the presheaf
1316	  tensor is right exact, so \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the locally-zero cokernel
1317	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\), which is therefore locally
1318	  zero (Mathlib \(\mathtt{isLocallySurjective\_whiskerLeft}\)). \emph{Local
1319	  injectivity} is \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. The two together
1320	  give \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
1321	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
1322	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf monoidal structure, which is an isomorphism
1323	  and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\).
1324	\end{proof}

1336	\begin{lemma}
1337	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
1338	[The \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal substrate (route~(e) target)]
1339	  \label{lem:jw_ismonoidal}
1340	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:whisker_of_W}
1341	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The sheafification localizer \(J.W\) on
1342	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{monoidal}: it carries an
1343	  instance
1344	  \[
1345	    J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal},
1346	  \]
1347	  whose two fields are the whiskering-stability lemmas \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}
1348	  (and which is multiplicative because sheafification inverts identities and
1349	  composites). Consequently, by Mathlib's
1350	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)
1351	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) applied to the already-monoidal category
1352	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\), the localization functor
1353	  \(L = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\) (which is the localization at
1354	  \(J.W\)), and the unit isomorphism \(L(\mathbf{1}) \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), the
1355	  category
1356	  \[
1357	    \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
1358	      \;=\; \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
1359	  \]
1360	  acquires a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure whose tensor is the substrate
1361	  \(\otimes_X\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}) and whose associator,
1362	  left/right unitors, braiding and \emph{all} coherence (pentagon, triangle,
1363	  naturalities) are derived by the API. This is the route~(e) target: the
1364	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors
1365	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso} are
1366	  then components of this derived monoidal structure rather than hand-assembled
1367	  composites.
1368	
1369	  This block states the route~(e) construction; it depends on the sole open obligation
1370	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. It mirrors Mathlib's
1371	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) construction one categorical level up.
1372	\end{lemma}
1373	
1374	\begin{proof}
1375	  \uses{lem:whisker_of_W}
1376	  Multiplicativity of \(J.W\) (closure under identities and composition) is part
1377	  of its being a localizer (\(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). The two
1378	  whiskering-stability fields of \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) are
1379	  exactly the left- and right-whiskering statements of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
1380	  Hence \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) holds. Sheafification of presheaves of
1381	  modules is a localization at \(J.W\) (the underlying identification of
1382	  sheafification with localization at the locally-bijective class), and the unit
1383	  presheaf sheafifies to \(\mathcal{O}_X\); feeding these to
1384	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) produces the \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
1385	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with all coherence derived, as in
1386	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}. No \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure enters
1387	  at any point.
1388	\end{proof}
