803	\begin{definition}\leanok
804	[Canonical base-change map on sheaves of modules]
805	  \label{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map}
806	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.canonicalBaseChangeMap}
807	  \uses{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
808	  Given a cartesian square of schemes
809	  \(\square = (g' : X' \to X,\ f' : X' \to S',\ f : X \to S,\ g : S' \to S)\)
810	  with \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) and \(g\) flat, the
811	  \emph{canonical base-change map} on sheaves of modules is the natural
812	  transformation \(g'^* f^* \mathcal{F} \longrightarrow f'^* g^* \mathcal{F}\)
813	  arising from the universal property of pullback. Encoded as a morphism
814	  of \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\)-valued sheaf functors on the pulled-back open
815	  cover; this is the morphism whose iso-status discharges
816	  \cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}(1) in the present
817	  finite-locally-free / quasi-coherent-of-finite-type setting.
818	\end{definition}
819	
820	\begin{theorem}\leanok
821	[Stalkwise base-change isomorphism on affines]
822	  \label{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_app_app_isIso}
823	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.canonicalBaseChangeMap_app_app_isIso}
824	  \uses{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map, thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
825	  For every affine open \(V \subseteq S'\) and every affine open
826	  \(U \subseteq X\) lying over \(V\), the local component
827	  \((\text{canonicalBaseChangeMap}\ \square).\text{app}\ \mathcal{F}.\text{app}\ U\)
828	  of \cref{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map} is an isomorphism. Proof
829	  reduces (Stacks 02KH(2)) to the affine-base case: \(H^0\) commutes with
830	  flat extension of scalars on a quasi-compact quasi-separated affine
831	  morphism.
832	\end{theorem}

834	\begin{theorem}\leanok
835	[Canonical base-change map is a sheaf isomorphism]
836	  \label{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_isIso}
837	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.canonicalBaseChangeMap_isIso}
838	  \uses{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_app_app_isIso}
839	  Globalising the stalk-wise statement
840	  \cref{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_app_app_isIso}: the natural
841	  transformation \(g'^* f^* \mathcal{F} \to f'^* g^* \mathcal{F}\) of
842	  \cref{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map} is an isomorphism of

847	\begin{definition}\leanok
848	[Tensor-presentation of the pullback-of-sections functor]
849	  \label{def:quot_pullback_app_isoTensor}
850	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback_app_isoTensor}
851	  \uses{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map}
852	  Given a morphism \(g : X \to Y\) of schemes, affine opens
853	  \(U \subseteq Y\), \(V \subseteq X\) with \(g(V) \subseteq U\), and a
854	  quasi-coherent sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{N}\) on \(Y\), the
855	  \emph{tensor-presentation iso} packages the canonical iso

865	  (Step 2, project-side typed sorry): the
866	  \texttt{IsBaseChange}-packaging step

874	[\(\Sigma\)-pair packaging: section-level LinearEquiv + intertwining]
875	  \label{def:pullback_app_isoTensor_sigma}

880	  Iter-188 Lane F-introduced \(\Sigma\)-pair packaging declaration that

889	                     \mathcal N) \;\simeq_{\Gamma(Y,U)}\;
890	                     \Gamma(X,V) \otimes_{\Gamma(Y,U)} \Gamma(N,V)),\;
891	      \forall x,\; f(1\otimes x)\;=\;\text{baseMap}\,g\,\mathcal N\,e\,x.

898	  comment block at

902	      iso-characterizing identity \texttt{\_step2\_apply} together with
903	      \texttt{inv\_hom\_id}): rewrites
904	      \((\text{step2}.\mathrm{inv}.\mathrm{app}\,\top)\bigl(\text{tilde.toOpen}\,TR\,\top\,(1 \otimes x)\bigr)\)
905	      as
906	      \(\text{baseMap}\,(\text{Spec.map}\,\varphi)\,(\widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal N, V)})\,e_1\,(\text{tilde.toOpen}\,\Gamma(\mathcal N, V)\,\top\,x)\).
907	    \item \textbf{Stage 2} (uses (N1) baseMap naturality of
908	      \cref{lem:baseMap_pullbackComp_apply} together with the
909	      \(\Sigma\)-pair \texttt{\_step1\_apply} identity): transports
910	      \(\text{step1}.\mathrm{inv}\) across

912	      \(\text{baseMap}\,(\text{Spec.map}\,\varphi)\,((\text{pullback}\,\mathtt{\_hV.fromSpec}).\mathrm{obj}\,\mathcal N)\,e_2\,(\text{baseMap}\,\mathtt{\_hV.fromSpec}\,\mathcal N\,e_3\,x)\).
913	    \item \textbf{Stage 3} (uses (N2) baseMap composition via
914	      \texttt{pullbackComp} of
915	      \cref{lem:baseMap_pullback_comp_apply}): collapses the nested
916	      pair of \texttt{baseMap}'s of Stage 2 into a single
917	      \(\text{baseMap}\,(\text{Spec.map}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{\_hV.fromSpec})\,\mathcal N\,e_4\,x\)
918	      along the composite morphism.
919	    \item \textbf{Stage 4} (uses (N3) baseMap transport via
920	      \texttt{pullbackCongr} of
921	      \cref{lem:baseMap_pullbackCongr_apply}): transports the

924	      = \mathtt{\_hU.fromSpec} \circ g\) (which holds by definition of
925	      \texttt{Scheme.Hom.appLE} on \(g\) with \(g(V) \subseteq U\)),
926	      producing
927	      \(\text{baseMap}\,(\mathtt{\_hU.fromSpec} \circ g)\,\mathcal N\,e_5\,x\).
928	    \item \textbf{Stage 5} (uses (N2) again,
929	      \cref{lem:baseMap_pullback_comp_apply}): re-decomposes the

940	      of the Σ-pair intertwining property.
941	  \end{itemize}
942	\end{definition}

18	% (that is A.2.c) and not responsible for the identity-component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
19	% (that is A.3).

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
78	  
79	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
80	  % Definition df:aPf + Definition df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard
81	  % functors are \emph{abelian-group}-valued, by construction; the relative
82	  % Picard functor is defined as a quotient \emph{group})
83	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
84	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
85	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
86	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
87	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
88	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
89	  %  [\dots]
90	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
91	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
92	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
93	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
94	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the Picard functors are abelian-group-valued, and the
95	  relative one is a quotient of abelian groups; cf.\ Stacks tag 01CR for the
96	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve and let \(T\) be a
98	  \(k\)-scheme. The Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is an abelian group under
99	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
100	  \[
101	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
102	  \]
103	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a group homomorphism. Its image
104	  \[
105	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T)
106	  \]
107	  is therefore a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\), and the quotient set of
108	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
109	  \[
110	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T
111	  \]
112	  inherits a canonical abelian-group structure under which the quotient map
113	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjective
114	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
115	\end{lemma}
116	
117	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a separate "proof" block
118	% for df:Pfs; the abelian-group structure is part of the definition (the word
119	% \emph{group} appears in df:aPf already, and df:Pfs is a quotient of the same
120	% abelian groups). The verbatim quotes for the statements above are therefore
121	% the same as for the lemma. The project-side proof is a structural restatement.
122	\begin{proof}
123	  
124	  The abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) under tensor product is
125	  standard (Stacks tag 01CR or Hartshorne II~\S 6): for line bundles
126	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}'\), the tensor product
127	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\) is again
128	  invertible, the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) is the unit, and
129	  the dual \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the
130	  inverse. Pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules sends
131	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (the structure sheaf is
132	  preserved by inverse image) and satisfies
133	  $\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} \mathcal{N}') \cong
134	   \pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^*\mathcal{N}'$
135	  by the standard tensor compatibility of pullback. Therefore \(\pi_T^*\)
136	  preserves the unit, sends inverses to inverses, and is multiplicative on
137	  tensor products; it is a homomorphism of abelian groups.
138	
139	  The image \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is then a subgroup. The quotient set
140	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) of
141	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries the canonical quotient
142	  abelian-group structure (Lagrange's theorem on cosets specialised to an
143	  abelian quotient): for \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}' \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\), set
144	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{L}'] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}']\);
145	  well-definedness follows from the subgroup condition. The canonical map
146	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is by construction a
147	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
148	\end{proof}

150	\section{The relative Picard presheaf as a group-valued presheaf}
151	\label{sec:relpic_groupvalued}
152	
153	We now refine the set-valued functor of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} into a
154	group-valued presheaf.
155	
156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Relative Picard presheaf]
158	  \label{def:rel_pic_sharp}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp}
160	  
161	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
162	  % Definition df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor)
163	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
164	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
165	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}

168	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
169	  % topologies  by

171	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
172	  %  \end{dfn}"

177	  \[

187	  \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\), this is exactly \(\Pic_{X/S}\) of df:Pfs.
188	\end{definition}

208	[Naturality respects the group structure]
209	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}
210	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial}
211	  
212	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
213	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is functorial
214	  % into abelian groups; the word \emph{functor} into the category of abelian
215	  % groups encodes both the identity / composition laws and the
216	  % group-homomorphism property of the restriction maps)
217	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
218	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
219	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
220	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of

227	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
228	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
229	  groups; the structure maps are group homomorphisms).}

239	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not write out a separate proof for
240	% functoriality; the structural assertion is part of the definition. No

259	  By \cref{thm:pullback_natural}, the set map \(g^\sharp\) is defined by
260	  \([\mathcal{L}] \mapsto [g_C^* \mathcal{L}]\), where

262	  of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(g_C\) preserves tensor products and the
263	  structure sheaf, hence the map
264	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) on Picard groups is

327	\section{\'Etale sheafification}
328	\label{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}
329	
330	The presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is generally not an \'etale (or even Zariski)
331	sheaf. The standard obstruction (Kleiman \S 2, L1292--L1302): for a \(k\)-scheme
332	\(T\) carrying a non-trivial line bundle whose pullback to \(C \times_k T\) becomes
333	non-trivial after an open cover, the Zariski separated-presheaf condition
334	fails. Therefore, to obtain a representable functor in the sense of
335	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}, one must replace
336	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) by its \emph{associated \'etale sheaf}. Kleiman \S 2 names
337	this sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) and singles it out as the functor that Kleiman's
338	main existence theorem \S 4 (th:main) represents.

512	\begin{theorem}[Canonical sheafification unit with universal property (forward-looking)]
513	  \label{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_unit_canonical}
514	
515	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
516	  % Def.~df:Pfs + the associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following
517	  % (the \'etale sheaf is characterised as the sheaf associated to the
518	  % relative Picard functor, via the natural map from a presheaf to its
519	  % associated sheaf --- i.e.\ the sheafification unit with its universal
520	  % property)
521	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1328).
522	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
523	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
524	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
525	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
526	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
527	  % topologies  by
528	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
529	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
530	  %  \end{dfn}
531	  %  [\dots]
532	  %  if the latter functor is one of the three just displayed,
533	  %  then it is a sheaf in the indicated topology; in fact, it is the sheaf
534	  %  associated to any one of its predecessors, and the map between them is
535	  %  the natural map from a presheaf to its associated sheaf, as is easy to
536	  %  check.  In particular, the three displayed sheaves are the sheaves
537	  %  associated to the absolute Picard functor \(\Pic_X\), as well as to the
538	  %  relative Picard functor \(\Pic_{X/S}\)."
539	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
540	  Def.~df:Pfs together with the associated-sheaf paragraph
541	  immediately following (the natural map from a presheaf to its
542	  associated sheaf, taken inside the category of abelian groups, is
543	  universal among presheaf morphisms from \(\Pic_{X/S}\) into \'etale
544	  sheaves of abelian groups).}
545	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. There
546	  exists a canonical morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
547	  \[
548	    \eta \;\colon\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\;
549	    \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}
550	  \]
551	  with the following universal property. For every \'etale sheaf
552	  \(\mathcal{F}\) of abelian groups on \((\Sch/k)\) and every morphism
553	  of presheaves of abelian groups
554	  \(\varphi : \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \mathcal{F}\), there is a unique
555	  morphism of \'etale sheaves of abelian groups
556	  \(\tilde\varphi : \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \mathcal{F}\) such that
557	  \(\tilde\varphi \circ \eta = \varphi\). Equivalently, the pair
558	  \(\bigl(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}, \, \eta\bigr)\) is initial among
559	  pairs \((\mathcal{F}, \, \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \mathcal{F})\)
560	  consisting of an \'etale sheaf of abelian groups on \((\Sch/k)\) and
561	  a morphism of presheaves of abelian groups from \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\)
562	  to \(\mathcal{F}\). In particular, the abelian-group structure on
563	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) at each \(k\)-scheme \(T\) is uniquely
564	  determined (up to canonical isomorphism) by the abelian-group
565	  structure on \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) and the universal property of
566	  \(\eta\).
567	\end{theorem}
568	
569	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs itself, Kleiman \S 2 does not
570	% supply a separate proof body --- the universal property is built
571	% into ``the sheaf associated to'' as a synonym for sheafification,
572	% as the verbatim quote above already makes plain. The project-side
573	% argument is the standard universal property of sheafification in a
574	% Grothendieck topology, transported through the colimit-preservation
575	% of \(\AddCommGroup \to \mathbf{Set}\).
576	\begin{proof}
577	
578	  In any Grothendieck topology, every presheaf has a canonical
579	  associated sheaf, and the canonical morphism from a presheaf to its
580	  associated sheaf is universal among morphisms from the presheaf into
581	  sheaves (Stacks tag 00WI; Kleiman \S 2, paragraph following
582	  df:Pfs). The \'etale site of \((\Sch/k)\) is a Grothendieck
583	  topology, so this applies in particular to \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) and
584	  yields the canonical set-valued sheafification
585	  \((\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^{\sim_\et}\) together with the universal arrow
586	  out of \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\). The forgetful functor
587	  \(\AddCommGroup \to \mathbf{Set}\) preserves and reflects the
588	  filtered colimits and finite limits used by the plus-construction,
589	  so performing the same plus-construction inside \(\AddCommGroup\)
590	  yields a sheaf of abelian groups whose underlying set-valued sheaf
591	  coincides with the set-valued sheafification; the abelian-group
592	  structure on the sheafification is transported back through this
593	  identification. The resulting morphism \(\eta\) is then by
594	  construction the universal arrow in the abelian-group-valued
595	  sheafification, which is the universal property in the form stated.
596	\end{proof}

701	\paragraph{Gate annotation (iter-198 refresh).}
702	The residual prover gate inside
703	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean} (the file
704	implementing the six declarations above) is \emph{not} on the
705	\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} carrier --- that typed sorry was closed
706	in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean} during
707	the iter-188 unpacking, where
708	\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} was concretised as the subtype
709	\(\{N : (\texttt{pullback}\ \pi_C\ \pi_T).\mathtt{Modules}\ \mathbin{|}\
710	\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\ N\}\) and
711	\texttt{pullbackAlongProjection} / \texttt{pullback\_pullback\_eq} were
712	filled axiom-cleanly using \texttt{Scheme.Modules.pullback},
713	\texttt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}, and
714	\texttt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}.
715	
716	The genuine residual gate is the abelian-group instance on the
717	quotient \texttt{Quotient (preimage\_subgroup πC πT)} (the file-local
718	sorry scaffold near \texttt{RelPicFunctor.lean} L205--L235, which the
719	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup} declaration is
720	pinned to). That instance in turn depends on a tensor-product
721	\texttt{AddCommGroup} structure on \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}:
722	forming the abelian-group law on isomorphism classes
723	\([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes L']\) requires a tensor-product operation
724	on the carrier and an inverse for invertibles. Mathlib at the pinned
725	commit \texttt{b80f227} ships
726	\texttt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj} (a tensor product at
727	the level of \texttt{PresheafOfModules}), but does \emph{not} expose a
728	monoidal-category structure on the higher-level
729	\texttt{Scheme.Modules} category; this is the gap explicitly flagged
730	in \texttt{LineBundlePullback.lean}, L344--L346, with the comment
731	``Mathlib ships \texttt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj} but no
732	monoidal structure on \texttt{Scheme.Modules} directly at
733	\texttt{b80f227}'' --- the \texttt{Scheme.Modules} monoidal-structure
734	gap.
735	
736	Modulo that single file-local sorry, the five other prover-gate
737	sorries at \texttt{RelPicFunctor.lean} L287--L482 (the bodies of
738	\texttt{PicSharp},
739	\texttt{PicSharp.functorial},
740	\texttt{PicSharp.presheaf},
741	\texttt{PicSharp.etSheaf}, and
742	\texttt{PicSharp.etSheaf\_group\_structure}) close axiom-cleanly: each
743	consumes \texttt{addCommGroup} as a typeclass hypothesis and threads
744	the resulting group structure through the standard Mathlib pattern
745	(\texttt{QuotientAddGroup} for the quotient, \texttt{Functor.mk} for
746	the presheaf packaging, \texttt{Quotient.lift} respecting the
747	\texttt{AddCommGroup} structure for the functorial action, and
748	\texttt{CategoryTheory.presheafToSheaf etaleTopology AddCommGrpCat}
749	for the \'etale sheafification with its abelian-group target).
750	Once the \texttt{addCommGroup} instance is provided, the file
751	collapses from six sorries to one, and the one remaining sorry is
752	gated only on the Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.Modules} monoidal-structure
753	gap rather than on any project-side mathematics (making it an
754	upstream Mathlib-build-mode task, not an A.1.c mathematical gap).
755	
756	The relationship with \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability} is captured by
757	the slogan: the FGA Picard scheme of A.2.c represents
758	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (Kleiman \S 4, th:main: ``\dots represents
759	\(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\)''), \emph{not} \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) directly. The \'etale
760	sheafification step of this chapter is precisely what makes the
761	representability theorem in A.2.c well-posed.

764	\label{sec:relpic_out_of_scope}

766	The following constructions are downstream of this chapter and live in
767	dedicated sibling chapters; this chapter does \emph{not} touch them:

769	  \item \textbf{Representability of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) by a scheme}
770	    (A.2.c, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) --- the
771	    Grothendieck--Mumford--Kleiman existence theorem is the next step but is
772	    not part of this chapter.

774	    \Pic_{(X/S)\zar} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{(X/S)\et} \hookrightarrow
775	    \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}$} (Kleiman \S 2, th:cmp) --- the chain of inclusions
776	    under \(\mathcal{O}_S \cong f_*\mathcal{O}_X\); we use only the
777	    \'etale-sheafified functor here, not the full comparison chain.
778	  \item \textbf{Identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)} (A.3) --- the open subgroup
779	    scheme cut out by Hilbert polynomials, treated in a downstream chapter.

781	    Route~A stack.

783	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is representable (A.2.c), its representing scheme
784	    inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from the abelian-group-valued
785	    target via the Yoneda embedding; that refinement is part of A.2.c, not
786	    of this chapter.
787	\end{itemize}

2345	\section{Sheaf internal-hom of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (the \(\otimes\)-inverse
2346	  infrastructure)}
2347	\label{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}
2348	
2349	The PRIMARY group law (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) needs a
2350	\(\otimes\)-inverse for every line bundle, supplied by
2351	\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} as the dual
2352	\(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\). The standard route
2353	constructs that dual as a special case of the \emph{sheaf internal hom} of
2354	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules; over a fixed ring the internal hom of \(R\)-modules is the
2355	linear-coyoneda module \(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,N = (M \to N)\), with dual
2356	\(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,\mathbf{1} = M^\vee\), but for the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
2357	no such
2358	internal-hom / monoidal-closed structure exists at the
2359	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\), \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\), or general-categorical
2360	level. This section decomposes the construction of the missing object into
2361	individually-formalizable sub-steps, following the slice presentation of the internal
2362	hom; each block names the intended Lean declaration and its dependencies. The
2363	specialisation that closes \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is recorded in
2364	\cref{rem:dual_discharges_inverse}; an alternative descent-theoretic route is noted in
2365	\cref{rem:dual_via_stack}.
2366	
2367	A structural point governs the whole construction. The naive rule
2368	\(U \mapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(M(U), N(U))\) is \emph{contravariant} in the
2369	restriction maps of \(M\) (a restriction \(M(U) \to M(V)\) for \(V \subseteq U\)
2370	induces precomposition \(\Hom(M(V), N(V)) \to \Hom(M(U), \cdot)\) in the wrong
2371	direction), so it is \emph{not} a presheaf in the covariant sense and cannot be
2372	sheafified like the tensor product \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}. The remedy is
2373	the \emph{slice} formula \(U \mapsto (M|_U \to N|_U)\) --- the module of morphisms of
2374	\emph{restricted} modules over the restricted ring --- whose presheaf functoriality
2375	(restrict a global-over-\(U\) morphism to a global-over-\(V\) morphism, for
2376	\(V \subseteq U\)) \emph{is} covariant. Constructing that slice presheaf, and
2377	proving it satisfies the sheaf condition, is the substance of the build.
2378	
2379	\begin{definition}
2380	
2381	\leanok
2382	[The global-ring module on morphisms of presheaves of modules (value module)]
2383	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
2384	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.homModule}
2385	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2386	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2387	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2388	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3508--L3514).
2389	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2390	  % "abelian groups. In addition, given an element
2391	  % $\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2392	  % and a section $f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)$ then we can define
2393	  % $f\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2394	  % by either precomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{F}|_U$
2395	  % or postcomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{G}|_U$ (it gives
2396	  % the same result). Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules."
2397	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2398	  (tag area 01CM): the section ring acts on a morphism of restricted modules by
2399	  post-composition with multiplication by the section.}
2400	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a base category \(\mathcal{C}\)
2401	  that has a terminal object \(T\), and let \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\).
2402	  The abelian group of morphisms \(M \longrightarrow N\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules
2403	  carries a canonical \(R(T)\)-module structure --- the \emph{morphism module}
2404	  \(\mathtt{homModule}\,M\,N\). A global scalar \(f \in R(T)\) acts on a morphism
2405	  \(\varphi : M \to N\) by post-composition with multiplication by \(f\),
2406	  \[
2407	    f \cdot \varphi \;:=\; m_f^{N} \circ \varphi,
2408	  \]
2409	  where \(m_f^{N} : N \to N\) is the \emph{multiplication-by-\(f\)} endomorphism of
2410	  \(N\): the endomorphism that, on each object \(U \in \mathcal{C}\), is multiplication
2411	  by the restriction \(f|_U \in R(U)\) of \(f\) along the unique morphism \(U \to T\)
2412	  to the terminal object. The module axioms hold because the assignment
2413	  \(f \mapsto m_f^{N}\) is a ring homomorphism \(R(T) \to \mathrm{End}(N)\) into the
2414	  endomorphism ring of \(N\) --- a product \(f \cdot g\) goes to the \emph{composite}
2415	  \(m_f^{N} \circ m_g^{N}\) of the two multiplication endomorphisms, the order in which
2416	  the factors compose being reversed by the fact that the scalar acts by
2417	  \emph{post}-composition --- and composition of morphisms is biadditive in the
2418	  preadditive category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). Distributivity and the
2419	  associativity of the action then follow from bilinearity of composition together with
2420	  the homomorphism property of \(f \mapsto m_f^{N}\). This is the genuinely new core of
2421	  the construction: Mathlib supplies the \emph{fixed}-ring internal hom
2422	  \(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,N = (M \to N)\) over a single base ring, but nothing equipping the
2423	  morphism group with the module structure for the \emph{varying} structure presheaf at
2424	  the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
2425	\end{definition}
2426	
2427	\begin{definition}
2428	
2429	\leanok
2430	[Slice specialisation of the morphism module]
2431	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}
2432	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.internalHomObjModule}
2433	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
2434	  Specialise \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} to a slice category. For an object
2435	  (open) \(U\) of \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\), restricting along the forgetful functor
2436	  \(\mathtt{Over.forget}\,U : \mathcal{C}/U \to \mathcal{C}\) --- the open-immersion
2437	  restriction \(M \mapsto M|_U\) --- carries \(M, N\) to presheaves of modules
2438	  \(M|_U, N|_U\) over the restricted structure ring on the slice \(\mathcal{C}/U\). The
2439	  slice \(\mathcal{C}/U\) has the terminal object \(\mathtt{Over.mk}\,(\mathrm{id}_U)\),
2440	  at which the restricted ring takes the value \(R(U)\); hence
2441	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} makes the morphism group
2442	  \(M|_U \longrightarrow N|_U\) an \(R(U)\)-module, the
2443	  \(\mathtt{internalHomObjModule}\). This \(R(U)\)-module \((M|_U \to N|_U)\) is exactly
2444	  the value \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}.
2445	\end{definition}
2446	
2447	\begin{definition}
2448	
2449	\leanok
2450	[Presheaf internal hom of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
2451	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
2452	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.internalHom}
2453	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:presheaf_internal_hom_value,
2454	        def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value, lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
2455	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2456	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2457	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3500--L3524).
2458	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2459	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
2460	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
2461	  % Consider the rule
2462	  % $$
2463	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
2464	  % $$
2465	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
2466	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
2467	  % abelian groups. In addition, given an element
2468	  % $\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2469	  % and a section $f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)$ then we can define
2470	  % $f\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2471	  % by either precomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{F}|_U$
2472	  % or postcomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{G}|_U$ (it gives
2473	  % the same result). Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
2474	  % We will denote this sheaf
2475	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})$.
2476	  % There is a canonical ``evaluation'' morphism
2477	  % $$
2478	  % \mathcal{F}
2479	  % \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
2480	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
2481	  % \longrightarrow
2482	  % \mathcal{G}.
2483	  % $$"
2484	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2485	  (tag area 01CM).}
2486	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on \(\Opens X\) and let
2487	  \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). The \emph{presheaf internal hom}
2488	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) is the presheaf of \(R\)-modules whose value on an open
2489	  \(U\) is the module of morphisms of restricted modules
2490	  \[
2491	    \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U)
2492	      \;:=\;
2493	    \mathtt{ModuleCat.of}\;\bigl(R(U)\bigr)\;\bigl(M|_U \longrightarrow N|_U\bigr),
2494	  \]
2495	  the \(R(U)\)-module of \(R|_U\)-linear morphisms \(M|_U \to N|_U\) of presheaves of
2496	  modules on \(U\), formed via the open-immersion restriction \(U \hookrightarrow X\).
2497	  This value module is the slice specialisation
2498	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value} of the morphism module
2499	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}.
2500	
2501	  Explicitly, \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) is the presheaf of \(R\)-modules assembled from
2502	  three pieces. \emph{(a) Value modules.} On each open \(U\) the value is the
2503	  \(R(U)\)-module \((M|_U \to N|_U)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} /
2504	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}. \emph{(b) Restriction maps.} For an
2505	  inclusion \(V \subseteq U\) the restriction map
2506	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U) \to \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(V)\) is the further-restriction map
2507	  \cref{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}, \(\varphi \mapsto \varphi|_V\), which
2508	  is additive and semilinear over the ring restriction \(R(g) : R(U) \to R(V)\)
2509	  induced by \(g : V \to U\) --- the compatibility datum that the presheaf-of-modules
2510	  assembly (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.mk}\), via \(\mathtt{ofPresheaf}\)) consumes.
2511	  \emph{(c) Functoriality.} Restriction along the identity \(U \to U\) is the identity
2512	  map, and restriction along a composite \(V \xrightarrow{g} U \xrightarrow{h} W\) of
2513	  inclusions \(V \subseteq U \subseteq W\) (the morphism \(g : V \to U\) followed by
2514	  \(h : U \to W\) in \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\)) is the composite of the two
2515	  restriction maps \(\mathcal{H}om(M,N)(W) \to \mathcal{H}om(M,N)(U) \to
2516	  \mathcal{H}om(M,N)(V)\); both identity and composition are inherited from the
2517	  functoriality of further restriction, since \(\mathtt{Over.map}\) is a functor. These three pieces
2518	  make \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) a genuine, covariant presheaf of \(R\)-modules; this
2519	  assembled presheaf is the construction target \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.internalHom}\).
2520	
2521	  The slice formula is forced by contravariance: the pointwise rule
2522	  \(U \mapsto \Hom_{R(U)}(M(U), N(U))\) varies \emph{against} the restriction maps of
2523	  \(M\) and so is not a covariant presheaf, whereas the module of morphisms of
2524	  \emph{restricted} objects \(M|_U \to N|_U\) restricts covariantly. Thus, unlike the
2525	  tensor product \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} --- which is covariant in the
2526	  restriction maps and sheafifies directly --- the internal hom must be built through
2527	  the slice presentation, and there is no presheaf-level dual to sheafify naively.
2528	\end{definition}
2529	
2530	\begin{lemma}
2531	
2532	\leanok
2533	[Restriction map of the presheaf internal hom]
2534	  \label{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
2535	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.restrictionMap}
2536	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value, def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}
2537	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2538	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2539	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3502--L3508).
2540	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2541	  % "Consider the rule
2542	  % $$
2543	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
2544	  % $$
2545	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
2546	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
2547	  % abelian groups."
2548	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2549	  (tag area 01CM): the rule \(U \mapsto \Hom(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)\), with its
2550	  further-restriction maps, is a (pre)sheaf of abelian groups.}
2551	  Let \(g : V \to U\) be a morphism of \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\) (an inclusion
2552	  \(V \subseteq U\) of opens), and let \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). There
2553	  is a restriction map
2554	  \[
2555	    \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U) \longrightarrow \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(V),
2556	    \qquad \varphi \longmapsto \varphi|_V,
2557	  \]
2558	  the \(\mathtt{restrictionMap}\), sending a morphism of restricted modules
2559	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) to its further restriction \(M|_V \to N|_V\). Concretely, the
2560	  functor \(\mathtt{Over.map}\,g : \mathcal{C}/V \to \mathcal{C}/U\) realises
2561	  ``restrict from \(U\) to \(V\)'', and \(\varphi|_V\) is \(\varphi\) pulled back along
2562	  it --- the further restriction of the same morphism of presheaves of modules. This
2563	  map is additive, and it is \emph{semilinear} over the ring restriction
2564	  \(R(g) : R(U) \to R(V)\): for \(f \in R(U)\),
2565	  \[
2566	    (f \cdot \varphi)\big|_V \;=\; R(g)(f) \cdot \bigl(\varphi|_V\bigr).
2567	  \]
2568	  This semilinearity is the compatibility datum that the presheaf assembly of
2569	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} (through \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.ofPresheaf}\) /
2570	  \(\mathtt{mk}\)) requires.
2571	\end{lemma}
2572	
2573	\begin{proof}
2574	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
2575	  Further restriction along \(\mathtt{Over.map}\,g\) is functorial, so it carries a
2576	  morphism \(M|_U \to N|_U\) to a morphism \(M|_V \to N|_V\) and respects identities and
2577	  composites; in particular the induced map on morphism groups is additive. For the
2578	  semilinearity, recall that the global-scalar action of
2579	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} acts on \(\varphi\) by post-composition with
2580	  the multiplication-by-\(f\) endomorphism \(m_f^{N}\); restricting that endomorphism
2581	  from \(U\) to \(V\) is multiplication by \(f|_V = R(g)(f)\) on \(N|_V\). Since further
2582	  restriction commutes with composition of morphisms, restricting \(f \cdot \varphi =
2583	  m_f^{N} \circ \varphi\) yields \(m_{R(g)(f)}^{N|_V} \circ (\varphi|_V) = R(g)(f) \cdot
2584	  (\varphi|_V)\), which is the asserted compatibility.
2585	\end{proof}
2586	
2587	\begin{definition}
2588	
2589	\leanok
2590	[Presheaf dual against the structure presheaf]
2591	  \label{def:presheaf_dual}
2592	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.dual}
2593	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
2594	  With \(R\) and \(M\) as in \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}, the
2595	  \emph{presheaf dual} of \(M\) is the internal hom into the unit (structure)
2596	  presheaf,
2597	  \[
2598	    M^\vee \;:=\; \mathcal{H}om(M, R),
2599	  \]
2600	  i.e.\ \(M^\vee(U) = \mathtt{ModuleCat.of}\,(R(U))\,(M|_U \to R|_U)\), the
2601	  \(R(U)\)-module of \(R|_U\)-linear functionals on \(M|_U\). This is the
2602	  presheaf-of-modules analogue of the linear dual
2603	  \(\mathtt{Module.Dual}\,R\,M = (M \to R)\) that serves as the \(\otimes\)-inverse of
2604	  an invertible module over a fixed ring; here the fixed-ring dual is taken
2605	  open-by-open and assembled by the slice presentation of
2606	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}.
2607	\end{definition}
2608	
2609	\begin{lemma}
2610	
2611	\leanok
2612	[Evaluation/contraction of the internal hom]
2613	  \label{lem:internal_hom_eval}
2614	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.internalHomEval}
2615	  % NOTE (iter-224): \texttt{PresheafOfModules.internalHomEval} is CLOSED axiom-clean
2616	  % (\(\{\texttt{propext}, \texttt{Classical.choice}, \texttt{Quot.sound}\}\)). The full
2617	  % morphism assembles the per-open contraction \texttt{internalHomEvalApp}
2618	  % (the \(R(U)\)-bilinear \((M|_U) \otimes_{R(U)} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R(U)\),
2619	  % \(s \otimes \varphi \mapsto \varphi(s)\)) sectionwise; naturality follows from the
2620	  % evaluate/restrict-commutation argument in the proof body. The iter-222/223 ``whnf
2621	  % heartbeat-bomb'' obstacle was a stale Mathlib-version artifact and is gone.
2622	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2623	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2624	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2625	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3517--L3524).
2626	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2627	  % "There is a canonical ``evaluation'' morphism
2628	  % $$
2629	  % \mathcal{F}
2630	  % \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
2631	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
2632	  % \longrightarrow
2633	  % \mathcal{G}.
2634	  % $$"
2635	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2636	  (tag area 01CM).}
2637	  With \(R\) and \(M\) as above, there is a canonical \emph{evaluation} (contraction)
2638	  morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules
2639	  \[
2640	    \mathrm{ev}_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_R M^\vee \;\longrightarrow\; R,
2641	    \qquad s \otimes \phi \longmapsto \phi(s),
2642	  \]
2643	  the counit of the tensor--hom adjunction
2644	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M \otimes_R -, R) \cong \mathcal{H}om(-, M^\vee)\) specialised at
2645	  \(R\). On each open \(U\) it is the open-by-open contraction
2646	  \((M|_U) \otimes_{R|_U} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R|_U\), \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto
2647	  \phi(s)\), and these are compatible with restriction, so they assemble to a morphism
2648	  of presheaves of modules. The tensor product is the presheaf tensor underlying
2649	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
2650	\end{lemma}
2651	
2652	\begin{proof}
2653	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
2654	  On a fixed open \(U\), the value \(M^\vee(U) = (M|_U \to R|_U)\) is the module of
2655	  \(R|_U\)-linear functionals on \(M|_U\), and the evaluation
2656	  \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto \phi(s)\) is the standard contraction
2657	  \((M|_U) \otimes_{R(U)} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R(U)\) bilinear over \(R(U)\), hence a
2658	  well-defined \(R(U)\)-linear map out of the tensor product. For \(V \subseteq U\)
2659	  the square relating the \(U\)- and \(V\)-contractions commutes because evaluating
2660	  then restricting equals restricting both arguments then evaluating
2661	  (\(\phi(s)|_V = (\phi|_V)(s|_V)\)); hence the open-by-open contractions are natural
2662	  in the restriction maps and define a morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules.
2663	  Concretely, the morphism is defined by specifying, on each open \(U\), the per-open
2664	  contraction \((M|_U) \otimes_{R(U)} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R(U)\), \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto \phi(s)\).
2665	  The single remaining datum is the naturality square, which reduces to the
2666	  evaluate/restrict-commutation identity \(\phi(s)|_V = (\phi|_V)(s|_V)\) above: the
2667	  restriction \(\phi|_V\) of a functional inside the dual \(M^\vee\) is its restriction
2668	  to the open \(V\), and restriction is functorial, so evaluating the restricted
2669	  functional on the restricted section agrees with restricting the value of the
2670	  evaluation. This is the same restriction-functoriality argument already used to make
2671	  the internal-hom restriction maps functorial in
2672	  \cref{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}. This is the counit of the tensor--hom
2673	  adjunction of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} specialised to the unit presheaf.
2674	\end{proof}
2675	
2676	\begin{lemma}
2677	
2678	\leanok
2679	[The internal hom is a sheaf; the sheaf-level dual]
2680	  \label{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}
2681	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual}
2682	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, lem:internal_hom_eval}
2683	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2684	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2685	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3502--L3514).
2686	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2687	  % "Consider the rule
2688	  % $$
2689	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
2690	  % $$
2691	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
2692	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
2693	  % abelian groups. ... Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules."
2694	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2695	  (tag area 01CM).}
2696	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(R = X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\), and let
2697	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with \(N\) a sheaf of modules. Then the
2698	  presheaf internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
2699	  satisfies the sheaf condition: for an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(U\), a family of
2700	  morphisms \(M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) agreeing on the overlaps
2701	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) glues to a unique morphism \(M|_U \to N|_U\), because \(N\) is a
2702	  sheaf and morphisms of sheaves of modules are determined and gluable section-wise.
2703	  Hence \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) descends to an object of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).
2704	  Specialising to \(N = \mathcal{O}_X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,R\) gives the
2705	  \emph{sheaf-level dual}
2706	  \[
2707	    \mathtt{dual}\,M \;:=\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X)
2708	    \;\in\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X,
2709	  \]
2710	  and the evaluation \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval} descends to a morphism of sheaves of
2711	  modules \(M \otimes_X \mathtt{dual}\,M \to \mathcal{O}_X\).
2712	\end{lemma}
2713	
2714	\begin{proof}
2715	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, lem:internal_hom_eval}
2716	  Fix an open \(U\) and an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(U\). A morphism
2717	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) is a section of \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) over \(U\); restricting it
2718	  to the \(U_i\) gives a compatible family. Conversely, given morphisms
2719	  \(\varphi_i : M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) with \(\varphi_i|_{U_i \cap U_j} =
2720	  \varphi_j|_{U_i \cap U_j}\), section-wise gluing in the sheaf \(N\) produces, for
2721	  each open \(W \subseteq U\) and section \(s \in M(W)\), a well-defined section of
2722	  \(N(W)\) by gluing the \(\varphi_i(s|_{W \cap U_i})\) (these agree on overlaps by
2723	  hypothesis, and \(N\) is a sheaf so the glued section is unique). The resulting
2724	  assignment is \(R(W)\)-linear and natural in \(W\), hence a morphism
2725	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) restricting to the \(\varphi_i\); uniqueness is the separatedness
2726	  of \(N\). This is exactly the sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\), so it is a
2727	  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Taking \(N = \mathcal{O}_X\) yields
2728	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,M\), and the evaluation morphism of \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval},
2729	  being a morphism of presheaves into the sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\), is a morphism of
2730	  the corresponding sheaves of modules.
2731	\end{proof}
2732	
2733	\begin{lemma}
2734	
2735	
2736	\leanok
2737	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with the linear dual (H2$'$)]
2738	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
2739	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoDualEquiv}
2740	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
2741	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M\) be an
2742	  \(S\)-module. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the formation of the
2743	  linear dual: the canonical map
2744	  \[
2745	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \to_S S\bigr)
2746	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2747	    \bigl(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e M \to_R R\bigr),
2748	    \qquad \varphi \;\longmapsto\; e^{-1} \circ \varphi,
2749	  \]
2750	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence, with inverse \(\psi \mapsto e \circ \psi\). On both
2751	  sides the \(R\)-module structure is the \(S\)-module structure of \(M\) pulled back
2752	  along \(e\), so that \(r \cdot m = e(r) \cdot m\): the left-hand side is the
2753	  \(S\)-linear dual \((M \to_S S)\) regarded as an \(R\)-module by restriction, and the
2754	  right-hand side is the \(R\)-linear dual of the restricted module
2755	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e M\) into the restricted ground ring. This is the dual
2756	  analogue of \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}: it is to the internal hom
2757	  / dual exactly what that lemma is to the tensor product. It is the \emph{H2$'$
2758	  ingredient} of the restriction-compatibility isomorphism of the C-bridge
2759	  \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} --- the ring-iso/dual commutation that, composed with
2760	  the open-immersion pushforward comparison H1, identifies the dual of a restriction
2761	  with the restriction of the dual.
2762	\end{lemma}
2763	
2764	\begin{proof}
2765	  The two assignments \(\varphi \mapsto e^{-1} \circ \varphi\) and
2766	  \(\psi \mapsto e \circ \psi\) are mutually inverse, since \(e \circ e^{-1} =
2767	  \mathrm{id}_S\) and \(e^{-1} \circ e = \mathrm{id}_R\) as ring homomorphisms, so the
2768	  two composites are the identity on functionals. Each assignment is \(R\)-linear:
2769	  additivity is immediate, and for a scalar \(r \in R\) the defining relation
2770	  \(r \cdot m = e(r) \cdot m\) of the pulled-back module structure makes each linearity
2771	  check a single rewrite --- \(e^{-1}\) (resp.\ \(e\)) intertwines the \(R\)-action on
2772	  the source with the \(R\)-action on the target precisely because \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\)
2773	  are inverse ring homomorphisms identifying \(R\) with \(S\). The forward map is
2774	  therefore an \(R\)-linear bijection, i.e.\ an \(R\)-linear equivalence; packaging the
2775	  two one-sided maps with the inverse identities gives
2776	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoDualEquiv}\).
2777	\end{proof}
2778	
2779	\begin{definition}
2780	
2781	\leanok
2782	[Precomposition equivalence on dual sections]
2783	  \label{def:presheaf_dual_precomp_equiv}
2784	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.dualPrecompEquiv}
2785	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual}
2786	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings and let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\)
2787	  be an isomorphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules. On a fixed open \(U\), precomposition
2788	  with \(e\) gives an \(R(U)\)-linear equivalence between the dual sections
2789	  \[
2790	    \mathtt{dualPrecompEquiv}\,e\,U \;\colon\;
2791	      \bigl(M'|_U \to R|_U\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \bigl(M|_U \to R|_U\bigr),
2792	      \qquad \varphi \;\longmapsto\; \varphi \circ e|_U,
2793	  \]
2794	  with inverse \(\psi \mapsto \psi \circ (e^{-1})|_U\). This is the section-level core of
2795	  the contravariant functoriality of the presheaf dual \(M \mapsto M^\vee\)
2796	  (\cref{def:presheaf_dual}) in isomorphisms: precomposing a functional on \(M'\) with
2797	  the comparison isomorphism produces a functional on \(M\), \(R(U)\)-linearly and
2798	  invertibly.
2799	\end{definition}
2800	
2801	\begin{definition}
2802	
2803	\leanok
2804	[The presheaf dual carries an iso \(M \cong M'\) to an iso \(M'^\vee \cong M^\vee\)]
2805	  \label{def:presheaf_dual_iso_of_iso}

2809	  canonical isomorphism of presheaf duals
2810	  \[
2811	    \mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\,e \;\colon\; M'^\vee \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; M^\vee
2812	  \]
2813	  --- note the reversal of direction, the dual being contravariant --- assembled
2814	  open-by-open from the precomposition equivalences
2815	  \(\mathtt{dualPrecompEquiv}\,e\,U\) of \cref{def:presheaf_dual_precomp_equiv}.
2816	  Naturality in \(U\), i.e.\ compatibility of these sectionwise equivalences with

2821	\begin{definition}
2822	
2823	\leanok
2824	[The sheaf-level dual carries an iso \(M \cong M'\) to an iso \(\mathtt{dual}\,M' \cong \mathtt{dual}\,M\)]
2825	  \label{def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso}
2826	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dualIsoOfIso}
2827	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, def:presheaf_dual_iso_of_iso}
2828	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) an isomorphism in
2829	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Then there is a canonical isomorphism of sheaf-level
2830	  duals
2831	  \[
2832	    \mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\,e \;\colon\; \mathtt{dual}\,M' \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;

2834	  \]
2835	  obtained by sheafifying the presheaf isomorphism \(\mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\,e\) of
2836	  \cref{def:presheaf_dual_iso_of_iso} through the sheaf-level dual of

2842	
2843	\begin{lemma}
2844	
2845	\leanok
2846	[Open-immersion slice-site sheaf equivalence (the shared slice bridge)]
2847	  \label{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}
2848	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overSliceSheafEquiv}
2849	  % SOURCE: Mathlib code-location provenance (this is NOT an external literature
2850	  % citation, so it carries no verbatim mathematical SOURCE QUOTE). This lemma
2851	  % completes the documented TODO at Mathlib/Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean, lines
2852	  % 19--22, reproduced here for orientation:
2853	  %   "show that both functors of the equivalence overEquivalence U are continuous
2854	  %    and induce an equivalence between Sheaf ((Opens.grothendieckTopology X).over U) A
2855	  %    and Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology U) A for any category A."
2856	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open subset, with associated open
2857	  immersion \(\iota : U \hookrightarrow X\). The reindexing equivalence
2858	  \(\mathtt{overEquivalence}\,U : \mathtt{Over}\,U \simeq \mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\)
2859	  --- between the slice of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) over \(U\) and the opens of the subspace
2860	  \(\widetilde{U}\) --- upgrades to an \emph{equivalence of sheaf categories}
2861	  \[
2862	    \mathtt{Sheaf}\bigl((\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,X).\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr)\,A
2863	      \;\;\simeq\;\;
2864	    \mathtt{Sheaf}\bigl(\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,U\bigr)\,A
2865	  \]
2866	  for any target category \(A\), compatible with the module-pullback comparison
2867	  \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). This completes the Mathlib TODO recorded at
2868	  \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean} (lines 19--22). It is the \emph{shared root}
2869	  underlying both the restriction-compatibility isomorphism for the dual
2870	  (\cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}) and the morphism-descent / gluing engine
2871	  (\cref{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}): both reduce to identifying a
2872	  slice-indexed family of sections over \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\) with an
2873	  open-immersion-indexed family over \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\), and that
2874	  identification is exactly this equivalence.
2875	\end{lemma}
2876	
2877	\begin{proof}
2878	  The functor \(\mathtt{overEquivalence}\,U\) realises \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\) as a
2879	  \emph{dense subsite} of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\): every open of the subspace
2880	  \(\widetilde{U}\) is covered by down-sets \(\{V : V \le U\}\), and these down-sets are
2881	  precisely the objects of the slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\). One therefore applies the
2882	  dense-subsite transfer \(\mathtt{Functor.IsDenseSubsite.sheafEquiv}\): a continuous,
2883	  cocontinuous, dense inclusion of sites induces an equivalence of the associated sheaf
2884	  categories. The continuity and cover-lifting hypotheses are supplied by the slice-site
2885	  instances of \(\mathtt{Sites/Over}\) (the Grothendieck topology
2886	  \(J.\mathtt{over}\,U\) and its comparison with the topology pulled back along
2887	  \(\iota\)).
2888	
2889	  The over-category coherence that is laborious for a general site --- the pseudofunctor
2890	  identities \(\mathtt{Over.mapId}\), \(\mathtt{Over.mapComp}\) and the associativity
2891	  \(\mathtt{overMapPullback\_assoc}\) --- is here \emph{automatic}: because
2892	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) is a thin poset, every hom-set is a subsingleton, so each such
2893	  square commutes by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) (and its heterogeneous form
2894	  \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.helim}\) where source and target objects differ only up to a
2895	  propositional equality of opens). The sole sectionwise content is the down-set
2896	  identification
2897	  \[
2898	    \iota_{!}\bigl(\iota^{-1}(V)\bigr) \;=\; V
2899	    \qquad (V \le U),
2900	  \]
2901	  i.e.\ the image under the open immersion of the preimage of an open contained in \(U\)
2902	  is that open again. Transporting sections along this equality and invoking the
2903	  dense-subsite equivalence yields the stated sheaf equivalence, compatible with
2904	  \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\) by the same thinness argument.
2905	\end{proof}

2948	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with
2949	  \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\). Then the sheaf-level dual
2950	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} is again locally trivial of

2954	\begin{proof}
2955	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
2956	        lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv, lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}
2957	  Local triviality of \(L\) furnishes, around each point, an open \(U\) with a
2958	  trivialisation \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The crux is the
2959	  restriction-compatibility isomorphism
2960	  \[
2961	    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)\big|_f \;\cong\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(L|_f\bigr)
2962	  \]
2963	  for an open immersion \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\). Its first three steps run exactly
2964	  as for the closed linchpin \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, because both
2965	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) and the tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) are of the form
2966	  \(\mathtt{sheafification}.\mathrm{obj}\) of a presheaf:
2967	  \begin{enumerate}
2968	    \item reduce restriction to the abstract pullback,
2969	      \((-)|_f \cong (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via
2970	      \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\);
2971	    \item move the pullback inside the sheafification,
2972	      \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi \cong
2973	      \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\), via
2974	      \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\);
2975	    \item strip the outer sheafification with \(\mathtt{.mapIso}\), reducing the claim
2976	      to the presheaf-level comparison.
2977	  \end{enumerate}
2978	  These three steps, together with the open-immersion pushforward comparison \emph{H1},
2979	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) (the identification of
2980	  two left adjoints of the same pushforward, \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\)
2981	  together with \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\)), are reused \emph{verbatim} from
2982	  the tensor and do typecheck for the dual.
2983	
2984	  Here the parallel with the tensor \emph{ends}. After H1 the residual to close is
2985	  \[
2986	    (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,A)
2987	      \;\cong\;
2988	    \mathtt{dual}\bigl((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,A\bigr),
2989	  \]
2990	  i.e.\ the open-immersion pushforward commutes with the presheaf dual. For the tensor
2991	  the corresponding residual closed through the ring-iso commutation, because
2992	  \(M \otimes_X N\) is \emph{sectionwise} \(M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)\) --- a literal
2993	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\)-image, to which the strong-monoidal comparison applies.
2994	  The dual is \emph{not} of this form: \(\mathtt{dual} = \mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
2995	  is the \emph{slice} internal hom, whose value at an open \(V\) is a module of
2996	  morphisms over the slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\), not a sectionwise
2997	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\)-image. Consequently the ring-iso/dual commutation
2998	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv} is only the \emph{ModuleCat-level shadow}
2999	  of the comparison; it does \emph{not} by itself discharge the residual, and the naive
3000	  ``verbatim mirror'' \emph{H2$'$} of the tensor's ring-iso step does not close it.
3001	
3002	  % NOTE (iter-230 review, EMPIRICALLY FALSIFIED — pending blueprint-writer rewrite):
3003	  % the paragraph below is WRONG. The iter-230 binding probe (live lean_goal /
3004	  % lean_multi_attempt) showed the post-H1 residual
3005	  %   (PresheafOfModules.pushforward β).obj (dual M.val) ≅ dual ((pushforward β).obj M.val)
3006	  % is PRESHEAF-level and VARYING-RING-module, and is NOT discharged by
3007	  % lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv (overSliceSheafEquiv), which is SHEAF-level and
3008	  % parametric in a FIXED value category. Three mismatches: sheaf-vs-presheaf;
3009	  % fixed-value-cat-vs-varying-ring module action; whole-U slice site vs per-open slices.
3010	  % The genuinely-needed ingredient is a NEW presheaf+module per-V slice-internal-hom
3011	  % comparison (f^* ℋom(A,B) ≅ ℋom(f^*A,f^*B) for an open immersion), ~150–300 LOC, NOT
3012	  % the existing shared root. See informal/dual_restrict_iso.md and the iter-228 NOTE above.
3013	  The residual is instead discharged by the shared slice-site sheaf equivalence
3014	  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}. Under that equivalence the slice internal
3015	  hom computed over \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\) inside \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) corresponds to the
3016	  slice internal hom computed over \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\); because
3017	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) is a thin poset, the comparison is precisely the poset-thin
3018	  reindexing of \(\mathtt{overEquivalence}\,U\), all of whose naturality squares commute
3019	  by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\). The ring-iso/dual commutation
3020	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv} survives only as the sectionwise algebraic
3021	  shadow carried along inside this transport. Combining the equivalence with the
3022	  trivialisation gives
3023	  \[
3024	    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U
3025	      \;\cong\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(L|_U\bigr)
3026	      \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
3027	      \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U,
3028	  \]
3029	  the last identification being evaluation at \(1\), the canonical iso of the dual of a
3030	  free rank-one module with itself. Hence \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) is locally trivial of
3031	  rank one.
3032	
3033	  No tensor stalk, and no whiskering \(M \triangleleft \eta\) of the sheafification
3034	  unit, is ever invoked: the comparison reduces to the open-immersion
3035	  \(\leftrightarrow\) slice transport of \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}
3036	  (a structural reindexing on a thin poset, not a stalk). (Equivalently, this is item~2
3037	  of Stacks Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible}, tag 01CR: the dual of an
3038	  invertible module is invertible.)
3039	\end{proof}

3041	\begin{lemma}
3042	
3043	\leanok
3044	[Local isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules glue to a global isomorphism]
3045	  \label{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}
3046	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_restrict}
3047	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi \colon M \to N\) a morphism in
3048	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\).
3049	  Suppose that for every point \(x \in X\) there is an open neighbourhood \(U_x\) of
3050	  \(x\) such that the restriction of \(\varphi\) to \(U_x\) --- the image
3051	  \((\mathtt{restrictFunctor}\,(U_x).\mathtt{i}).\mathtt{map}\,\varphi\) under the
3052	  restriction functor along the open immersion \((U_x).\mathtt{i}\colon U_x \to X\) ---
3053	  is an isomorphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,U_x\). Then \(\varphi\) is an
3054	  isomorphism.
3055	\end{lemma}
3056	
3057	\begin{proof}
3058	  Being an isomorphism for a morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules can be
3059	  checked on the underlying morphism of sheaves of abelian groups, and there it can be
3060	  checked stalkwise. Fix a point \(x \in X\). Choose a preimage \(x' \in U_x\) of \(x\)
3061	  under the open immersion \((U_x).\mathtt{i}\). By hypothesis the restriction
3062	  \((\mathtt{restrictFunctor}\,(U_x).\mathtt{i}).\mathtt{map}\,\varphi\) is an
3063	  isomorphism, hence so is its image under any functor --- in particular under the
3064	  stalk-at-\(x'\) functor on the underlying ab-sheaves. The natural isomorphism
3065	  ``restriction commutes with stalks'' (\(\mathtt{restrictStalkNatIso}\)) identifies
3066	  this with the stalk of (the underlying ab-sheaf morphism of) \(\varphi\) at the image
3067	  point \(x\); transporting along it shows that stalk is an isomorphism. As this holds
3068	  at every \(x\), and a morphism of sheaves of abelian groups all of whose stalks are
3069	  isomorphisms is itself an isomorphism (stalkwise iso-detection,
3070	  \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_stalkFunctor\_map\_iso}\) on \(\mathtt{TopCat.Sheaf}\)), the
3071	  underlying ab-sheaf morphism of \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism. Finally the forgetful
3072	  functor \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules.toPresheaf}\) reflects isomorphisms, so \(\varphi\)
3073	  itself is an isomorphism.
3074	\end{proof}

3076	\begin{definition}
3077	
3078	
3079	\leanok
3080	[Scheme-level linearity wrapper \(\mathtt{homMk}\) (A-bridge step (ii))]
3081	  \label{def:scheme_modules_homMk}
3082	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homMk}
3083	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Given a morphism
3084	  \(g \colon M.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf} \to N.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf}\) of
3085	  the underlying presheaves of abelian groups, together with a hypothesis \(hg\) that
3086	  \(g\) is \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear sectionwise, \(\mathtt{homMk}\,g\,hg\) is the
3087	  morphism \(M \to N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) that this data determines. It
3088	  is a thin wrapper that packages \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.homMk}\) at the scheme
3089	  level: the sectionwise-linearity datum promotes the ab-group morphism to a genuine
3090	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Its \(\mathtt{@[simp]}\) companion
3091	  \(\mathtt{toPresheaf\_map\_homMk}\) records that forgetting back recovers \(g\), i.e.\
3092	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,X).\mathtt{map}\,(\mathtt{homMk}\,g\,hg) = g\). This is step
3093	  (ii) of the A-bridge \cref{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}: once the
3094	  underlying ab-sheaf morphism has been glued, \(\mathtt{homMk}\) re-attaches the
3095	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linearity to produce the global morphism of modules.
3096	\end{definition}
3097	
3098	\begin{lemma}
3099	[Compatible local morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules glue to a global morphism]
3100	  \label{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}
3101	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homOfLocalCompat}
3102	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and \(\{U_i\}\) an
3103	  open cover of \(X\). Suppose given, for each \(i\), a morphism
3104	  \(f_i \colon M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,U_i\) such that
3105	  for all \(i, j\) the restrictions of \(f_i\) and \(f_j\) to \(U_i \cap U_j\) agree.
3106	  Then there is a unique global morphism \(f \colon M \to N\) in
3107	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) whose restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(f_i\).
3108	\end{lemma}
3109	
3110	\begin{proof}
3111	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_homMk, lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}
3112	  A morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is, concretely, a morphism of the
3113	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups that is in addition \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear
3114	  sectionwise. We build \(f\) in two steps.
3115	
3116	  \emph{(i) Glue the underlying ab-sheaf morphism.} Forget \(M\) and \(N\) to their
3117	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups. The morphisms-between-them presheaf
3118	  \(\mathcal{H} := \mathtt{presheafHom}\,(M.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf})\,
3119	  (N.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf})\), whose value at an open \(W\) is the abelian
3120	  group of morphisms \(M|_W \to N|_W\) of presheaves of abelian groups, is itself a
3121	  \emph{sheaf of types} --- this is the ``hom into a sheaf is a sheaf'' principle
3122	  \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsSheaf.hom}\), which consumes the sheaf condition of \(N\).
3123	  Regarding \(\mathcal{H}\) as a \(\mathtt{TopCat.Sheaf}\), its universal gluing
3124	  property \(\mathtt{existsUnique\_gluing}\) amalgamates any compatible family of local
3125	  sections of \(\mathcal{H}\) into a unique global section. This route is preferred over
3126	  the raw sieve-level amalgamation \(\mathtt{presheafHom\_isSheafFor}\), which carries
3127	  strictly more bookkeeping.
3128	
3129	  The load-bearing datum is, for each \(i\), the \emph{local section}
3130	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i \in \mathcal{H}.\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathrm{op}\,U_i)\)
3131	  manufactured from \(f_i\). Its component at a pair \((V, h : V \to U_i)\) is the given
3132	  component of \(f_i\), conjugated by the canonical \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\) identifications
3133	  that arise from the open-set equality \(\iota_i\bigl(\iota_i^{-1}(V)\bigr) = V\) valid
3134	  for \(V \subseteq U_i\) (the image under the open immersion \(\iota_i : U_i \to X\) of
3135	  the preimage of \(V\) is \(V\) again). The genuine coherence risk of the whole
3136	  construction is concentrated in the \emph{naturality field} of
3137	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i\): the requirement that these
3138	  \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugated components of \(f_i\) commute across the morphisms of
3139	  the slice category \(\mathtt{Over}\,U_i\). This is the one sub-piece that should be
3140	  built and verified first, as a standalone axiom-clean lemma.
3141	
3142	  This \(\mathtt{localSection}\) naturality is precisely the \emph{section-direction
3143	  slice} of the comparison packaged in \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}:
3144	  converting the local datum \(f_i\), which lives over the open-immersion pullback
3145	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U_i}\), into a section of \(\mathcal{H}\) indexed over the
3146	  slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,U_i\) is the one-sided realisation of the open-immersion
3147	  \(\leftrightarrow\) slice transport. As there, the \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugation is
3148	  controlled by the down-set identity \(\iota_i(\iota_i^{-1}(V)) = V\) for
3149	  \(V \subseteq U_i\), and the coherence is automatic on the thin poset
3150	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\). The dual restriction-iso
3151	  (\cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}) consumes the \emph{functor-direction} face of the
3152	  same equivalence; that the two share \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv} as a
3153	  common root is why the shared bridge, once built, unblocks both at once.
3154	
3155	  The remaining four sub-steps are mechanical: (a) the
3156	  cocycle/\(\mathtt{IsCompatible}\) condition on the family
3157	  \((\mathtt{localSection}\,i)_i\), which is exactly the assumed agreement of \(f_i\)
3158	  and \(f_j\) on the overlaps \(U_i \cap U_j\); (b) gluing via
3159	  \(\mathtt{existsUnique\_gluing}\) and converting the amalgamated section over
3160	  \(\mathrm{op}\,\top\) into a genuine morphism \(g \colon M.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf}
3161	  \to N.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf}\) of the underlying presheaves --- the
3162	  \(\top\)-section-to-morphism passage being the global-sections equivalence
3163	  \(\mathtt{presheafHomSectionsEquiv}\) / \(\mathtt{sheafHomSectionsEquiv}\) of the
3164	  hom-sheaf \(\mathcal{H}\) (which, since \(\top\) is terminal in \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\),
3165	  lands the glued section as an honest morphism of presheaves), \emph{not} a hand-unfold
3166	  of the \(\top\)-section; (c) the
3167	  sectionwise linearity check; and (d) the recovery that the restriction of \(g\) to
3168	  each \(U_i\) is the ab-sheaf morphism underlying \(f_i\).
3169	
3170	  \emph{(ii) Promote to \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear.} The linearity equation
3171	  \(g(r \cdot m) = r \cdot g(m)\) is a sectionwise identity between two sections of a
3172	  separated presheaf. It holds on each \(U_i\), because there \(g\) restricts to the
3173	  module morphism \(f_i\); since the two sides agree on the cover \(\{U_i\}\) and the
3174	  ambient presheaf is separated, they agree globally. Packaging \(g\) together with this
3175	  global linearity through the scheme-level wrapper \cref{def:scheme_modules_homMk}
3176	  (\(\mathtt{homMk}\)) yields a morphism \(f \colon M \to N\) in
3177	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\); its restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(f_i\) and its
3178	  uniqueness are both inherited from the ab-sheaf amalgamation of step (i).
3179	
3180	  This gluing engine is substantially larger than a routine glue: it is dominated by the
3181	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\) naturality field and the convert-and-recover bookkeeping, and
3182	  the naturality is the section-direction slice of
3183	  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv} --- so its true cost is bounded below by
3184	  building that shared bridge, not by the surrounding assembly. It is therefore to be
3185	  scoped as a multi-piece build --- with \(\mathtt{localSection}\), together with its
3186	  naturality, built as a standalone axiom-clean lemma first --- rather than a one-shot.
3187	  The step computes no tensor stalk --- it is purely a statement about gluing morphisms
3188	  of sheaves --- and so it does not re-enter the abandoned tensor-stalk commutation.
3189	\end{proof}

3191	\begin{remark}
3192	[How the dual infrastructure discharges the \(\otimes\)-inverse]
3193	  \label{rem:dual_discharges_inverse}
3194	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible, lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial,
3195	        lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat, lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict,
3196	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
3197	  Once \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom,def:presheaf_dual,%
3198	  lem:internal_hom_isSheaf,lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} are available,
3199	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is discharged with no further missing object:
3200	  take \(L^{-1} := \mathtt{dual}\,L\) (\cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}), which is a line
3201	  bundle by \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}. The global contraction \(\varepsilon_L
3202	  \colon L \otimes_X L^{-1} \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{not} obtained by sheafifying
3203	  the presheaf-level evaluation; it is instead built by \emph{gluing the canonical local
3204	  contraction morphisms}. On each trivialising open \(U\), the
3205	  restriction-compatibility iso \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} carries
3206	  \((L \otimes_X L^{-1})|_U\) to \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U\), and the
3207	  canonical local contraction \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U \to \mathcal{O}_U\)
3208	  of the free rank-one module with its dual is the left unitor
3209	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, an isomorphism. These local contractions are canonical,
3210	  hence agree on overlaps, and so glue --- by the A-bridge
3211	  \cref{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat} --- to a single global morphism
3212	  \(\varepsilon_L \colon L \otimes_X L^{-1} \to \mathcal{O}_X\). Since ``being an
3213	  isomorphism'' is local on \(X\) and \(\varepsilon_L\) restricts to an isomorphism on
3214	  each \(U\), it is a global isomorphism \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim}
3215	  \mathcal{O}_X\) by the B-connector \cref{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}.
3216	
3217	  This descent construction deliberately avoids the forbidden
3218	  ``sheafify-the-evaluation'' route --- which would re-enter the abandoned
3219	  tensor-stalk / sheafification-unit whiskering commutation --- and computes no tensor
3220	  stalk anywhere. It is the realization the ``infrastructure-blocked'' note in
3221	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} points to.
3222	\end{remark}

3224	\begin{remark}
3225	[Alternative route via object-level descent (fallback)]
3226	  \label{rem:dual_via_stack}
3227	  Should the slice-and-sheafify route of
3228	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom,lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} bottom out, the dual can
3229	  instead be assembled by \emph{object-level descent}: glue the local trivial duals
3230	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) along the inverse-transpose transition isomorphisms
3231	  \(g_{ij}^{-\mathsf{T}}\) of \(L\) into a global \(L^{-1}\) on \(X\). Abstractly this
3232	  is effective descent for sheaves of modules on the small Zariski site --- the
3233	  essential-surjectivity of the descent-data functor of an \(\mathtt{IsStack}\)
3234	  pseudofunctor \(U \mapsto (\mathcal{O}_U\text{-modules})\). This is the more
3235	  principled and reusable construction but the larger build (it requires constructing
3236	  the restriction pseudofunctor and proving the stack/effective-descent property),
3237	  and is recorded only as a fallback; the slice internal-hom route above is the
3238	  intended one. It is the same morphism-/object-level descent family on which the
3239	  associator re-route of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} also waits.
3240	\end{remark}

23

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
51	
52	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
53	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
54	
55	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
56	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
57	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
58	Lean substrate
59	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
60	outside the Picard context.
